text: A small SaaS startup wants to deploy a stateless web tier across multiple zones in a single region so a zonal outage doesn't take down the service. The team uses Compute Engine VMs. Which option achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: A single large VM with hourly snapshots restored manually after a failure
A regional managed instance group (MIG) with an instance template, fronted by a regional load balancer
Three independent VMs in three regions with DNS round-robin
AWS Auto Scaling Groups peered into the GCP VPC
explanation: A regional MIG spreads instances across zones automatically, recreates failed VMs, and integrates with load balancing — the canonical zonal-HA pattern. A single VM with snapshots cannot meet zonal HA. Multi-region DNS round-robin is overkill (and inconsistent) for a zonal-failure requirement. AWS ASGs are not a GCP service.

text: A media company plans to launch a new global streaming service and wants to ensure they can recover from a regional outage with an RPO of 5 minutes and RTO of 30 minutes for their primary user metadata database. The database has strong consistency and transactional requirements. Which Google Cloud service best meets these business continuity requirements with minimal operational overhead?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with cross-region read replicas and manual failover
Bigtable with replication across two regions
Spanner configured as a multi-region instance
Firestore in Native mode with a single regional location
explanation: Spanner multi-region instances provide strong consistency, transactional semantics, and automatic synchronous replication across regions with an RPO of zero and RTO measured in seconds — well within the stated requirements. Cloud SQL replicas are asynchronous (RPO > 0) and require manual failover. Bigtable is non-relational (no transactions across rows). A single-region Firestore deployment fails the regional-outage requirement.

text: A regulated EU customer requires that all data for their workload be stored and processed only within the EU, and that Google personnel cannot access the customer's data without explicit, audited approval per access. Which combination of controls best meets this requirement?
options: Sign a BAA, deploy to EU regions, and enable Cloud Audit Logs Data Access for all Google admin actions
Assured Workloads for EU + Access Approval + CMEK with Cloud EKM keys held by the customer
Pin resources to EU regions and use CMEK with Cloud HSM-backed keys held inside Google's KMS
VPC Service Controls perimeter around EU projects with default Google-managed encryption keys
explanation: Assured Workloads enforces region pinning and personnel/support residency. Access Approval requires explicit customer approval (with reason logged) before any Google access. CMEK with EKM keeps the key under customer control.

text: Your team is designing a new application that must scale to handle unpredictable bursts of HTTP traffic and scale to zero during quiet periods to optimize cost. The team wants the simplest deployment model. Which compute platform should you recommend?
options: Compute Engine managed instance group with autoscaling based on CPU utilization
GKE Standard cluster with the cluster autoscaler
Cloud Run service
App Engine standard environment with manual scaling
explanation: Cloud Run scales from zero to thousands of instances based on concurrent requests, bills per request and CPU/memory used during request processing, and has the simplest deployment model of the choices. MIGs do not scale to zero. GKE has cluster overhead even when idle. App Engine standard with manual scaling is the opposite of what's required.

text: A team needs a managed Kubernetes platform but doesn't want to manage node pools, upgrades, autoscaling, or worry about per-node sizing. They have variable, unpredictable workloads and want to pay only for pod requests. Which option fits best?
options: GKE Standard with cluster autoscaler
GKE Autopilot
Cloud Run for Anthos
GKE on VMware (Anthos clusters on bare metal)
explanation: GKE Autopilot manages the entire node infrastructure, charges per pod resource requests, and is Google's recommended default for new GKE workloads.

text: A financial-services workload must run on Google Cloud with these constraints: (1) one component requires privileged init containers to tune kernel networking parameters; (2) it needs reliable access to NVIDIA GPUs; (3) the team is willing to use Spot capacity but must receive preemption signals to drain in-flight work; (4) they want to avoid managing individual VMs and OS patching. Which compute platform is the BEST fit?
options: Cloud Run (2nd-gen execution environment) with GPU support and concurrency tuning
GKE Standard with a dedicated GPU node pool using Spot VMs and a node-termination handler
GKE Autopilot with GPU Spot Pods
Compute Engine Spot VMs with a custom container runtime managed by the team
explanation: GKE Standard is the only option that satisfies ALL four constraints: privileged init containers are allowed, GPU node pools are first-class, Spot VMs surface a preemption notice, and Google manages the control plane while node OS patching is largely automated via auto-upgrade. Autopilot disallows privileged containers. Cloud Run does not support privileged init containers and has stricter GPU/runtime constraints. Plain Compute Engine forces the team to own the runtime and patching.

text: You need to share a single VPC across multiple application teams' projects so the network team controls the network while application teams retain ownership of their projects. What is the canonical pattern?
options: VPC Network Peering between each application project's VPC
Network Connectivity Center hub with each project as a spoke
Shared VPC: a host project owns the VPC; service projects attach to it
Place all teams' resources in a single project and separate by labels
explanation: Shared VPC is the purpose-built pattern for centralized network ownership with decentralized application ownership.

text: An on-prem MySQL 8.0 database must be migrated to Cloud SQL for MySQL with minimal downtime. The team needs to validate the target for several days while the source is still live, then cut over during a planned window. Which Google Cloud service supports this with continuous change capture?
options: Storage Transfer Service with a recurring schedule reading mysqldump output written to Cloud Storage by an on-prem cron job
Database Migration Service (DMS) using continuous (CDC) migration mode with promotion at the planned cutover window
Cloud Composer DAG that runs mysqldump every 5 minutes and re-imports the full dataset into Cloud SQL using a Python operator
BigQuery Data Transfer Service for MySQL with a CDC-style scheduled pull from the source binary log into BigQuery, then export to Cloud SQL
explanation: DMS performs an initial dump and then streams binlog changes from the source, keeping Cloud SQL in near-sync until promotion — the standard low-downtime MySQL/PostgreSQL pattern. Storage Transfer Service moves objects, not relational change streams. Repeated full mysqldumps cannot meet the downtime/validation goal and overwhelm the source. BigQuery DTS targets analytics sources and does not provide a Cloud SQL CDC path.

text: An on-premises application needs to publish events to a Google Cloud event bus that will be consumed by Cloud Run services. Throughput will be ~50,000 events/second with bursts to 200,000/second. The publisher must use a private network path (no public internet). What is the recommended architecture?
options: HA VPN or Interconnect to the VPC; publisher uses a Private Service Connect endpoint for Pub/Sub; Cloud Run push subscriptions
Site-to-Site VPN to the VPC; publisher writes to a Cloud Storage bucket; Eventarc fans out finalize events to Cloud Run subscribers
Direct Pub/Sub publish over the public internet using API keys, with VPC Service Controls protecting the topic from non-allowlisted IPs
Interconnect to the VPC; publisher writes to BigQuery streaming inserts; a Dataflow pipeline forwards records to Cloud Run subscribers
explanation: Pub/Sub easily handles the throughput. Private Service Connect creates a private endpoint for the Pub/Sub API inside your VPC, giving the on-prem publisher (reachable via Interconnect or HA VPN) a private network path. Cloud Run push subscriptions are idiomatic for event-driven workloads.

doc_links.title: Private Service Connect for Google APIs

text: A retail company is planning to migrate ~600 VMs to Google Cloud over 9 months. They want a discovery + assessment tool that produces TCO analysis, dependency mapping, and migration wave planning before they touch any infrastructure. Which Google Cloud service should be their first stop?
options: Migrate to Virtual Machines
Migration Center
Database Migration Service
Storage Transfer Service
explanation: Migration Center is the unified discovery, assessment, and planning tool: TCO, fits, dependency maps, and wave plans. Migrate to VMs is the *execution* tool used after planning.

text: After completing assessment in Migration Center, your team is ready to lift-and-shift ~200 VMware VMs to Google Cloud with the smallest possible application changes and the ability to test cutover before final switch. Which execution tool is the right fit?
options: Migrate to Virtual Machines (m2VM) with replication and test clones
Database Migration Service
Storage Transfer Service
Manually rebuild each VM with Cloud Build templates
explanation: Migrate to Virtual Machines is purpose-built for VM lift-and-shift: it replicates source disks, lets you launch test-clone VMs in GCE, and supports a controlled cutover. DMS is for databases; STS is for objects; manual rebuilds defeat lift-and-shift.

text: An enterprise has 1,200 VMs on-prem (mixed Linux/Windows), 40 Oracle DBs, ~6 PB of unstructured files, and a SAP S/4HANA estate. They need a 24-month phased migration with: Migration Center for assessment + wave planning, an execution tool for VMs, a managed path for Oracle (no app changes), and a continuous on-prem→GCS path for the file estate during the long tail. Which combination is BEST?
options: Migration Center; Migrate to VMs; Bare Metal Solution for Oracle; Storage Transfer Service for files; certified SAP on Google Cloud program
Migration Center; rehost VMs on Cloud Run; convert Oracle to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL; gsutil one-shot copy for files; SAP on Compute Engine
Database Migration Service for both VMs and Oracle; Storage Transfer Service for files; SAP rebuilt as Cloud Run microservices over 24 months
Skip Migration Center; `gcloud compute import` for VMs; migrate Oracle to Spanner; manual rsync for files; SAP rehosted on a GKE Autopilot cluster
explanation: Bare Metal Solution is the canonical zero-app-change path for Oracle on GCP; Migrate to VMs handles the VM fleet; STS with the on-prem agent is the supported continuous file-copy path; SAP has a dedicated Google Cloud certified migration program. DMS is for relational databases only — it cannot migrate VMs. Cloud Run/Spanner aren't drop-in replacements for VMs/Oracle.

text: Where in the Google Cloud resource hierarchy should you set an organization policy that you want every project to inherit by default?
options: On each project individually after it is created
At the Organization node, with optional folder/project overrides
On the billing account
In a Cloud Storage bucket labeled 'policies'
explanation: Org policies set at the Organization node are inherited by all folders and projects below; folders/projects can override where allowed. Per-project setup defeats inheritance and is error-prone.

text: Your engineering org uses many GCP projects organized under folders by business unit. You want a base set of guardrails (org policies, audit log sinks, billing alerts) that every new folder/project inherits, with the ability to layer team-specific exceptions. What is the right primitive?
options: Apply the guardrails as IAM allow/deny policies attached to each project's default Compute Engine service account at provisioning time
Use the Resource Hierarchy: set base org policies and aggregated log/billing sinks at the Organization node, fine-tune at folders, and allow exceptions at projects
Maintain a runbook the platform team applies manually after each new project is created, with quarterly drift reviews via Cloud Asset Inventory
Use Cloud Asset Inventory to detect drift after the fact and trigger a Cloud Function to reconcile each project back to a baseline JSON in Cloud Storage
explanation: The Resource Hierarchy (Org → Folders → Projects) is precisely designed for inherited policy with overrides at any lower level. IAM on a default SA cannot enforce org-wide constraints like external-IP denial or log routing. Manual runbooks do not scale and create drift. Reconciling-after-the-fact leaves a noncompliance window and reinvents Org Policy poorly.

text: A company runs prod, staging, and dev across 4 business units. Requirements: (1) prod must be isolated from non-prod in the IAM blast radius; (2) BU finance leads must own their BU's billing and IAM but not other BUs'; (3) security team needs read-only across everything; (4) common shared services (logging, networking) must be reusable across all BUs. Which folder layout BEST fits?
options: Flat: every project directly under the Org node, with IAM granted per project and one shared billing account for the whole org
BU folders with `prod/` and `nonprod/` subfolders; a `common/` folder for shared host projects; org policies on `prod/`; per-BU billing subaccounts
Environment folders (`prod/`, `staging/`, `dev/`) at the top level with all BU projects mixed inside each, billing tracked per project label
A separate Google Cloud Organization per BU, peered via cross-org Shared VPC and a central log-aggregation project for shared services
explanation: BU folders provide IAM isolation and per-BU ownership; the `prod/` subfolder is the natural attach point for stricter org policies and IAM. A `common/` folder hosts Shared VPC and log sinks reusable by every BU. Org-level read role gives security the cross-cutting view. Flat or env-only layouts can't satisfy (1) and (2) simultaneously; separate Orgs fragment governance and shared services.

text: An enterprise needs a private Layer-2 connection to Google Cloud with sustained 100 Gbps capacity and no traffic over the public internet. Which product fits?
options: HA VPN with two tunnels
Dedicated Interconnect
Cloud NAT with a reserved external IP
Azure ExpressRoute
explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides 10/100 Gbps physical links into Google's network with no internet traversal. HA VPN runs over the public internet and is bandwidth-limited per tunnel (~3 Gbps). Cloud NAT only translates outbound VM traffic. ExpressRoute is a Microsoft Azure product.

text: You need to provide a 99.99% SLA-backed encrypted tunnel between an on-premises data center and a Google Cloud VPC, with dynamic routing. Which configuration meets the requirement?
options: Classic VPN with two tunnels
HA VPN gateway with two tunnels and BGP via Cloud Router
A single VPN tunnel with static routes
Cloud Interconnect Dedicated only — VPN cannot meet 99.99%
explanation: HA VPN provides a 99.99% SLA when configured with two tunnels on different interfaces, paired with BGP via Cloud Router.

text: An on-prem data center connects to a GCP VPC via two 10 Gbps Dedicated Interconnects in different metro facilities. Requirements: (1) 99.99% SLA for production traffic; (2) failover MUST be automatic with sub-minute convergence; (3) routing must prefer the lower-latency link; (4) plan must survive a full metro outage. Which design is BEST?
options: Place both 10 Gbps Dedicated Interconnects in the same metro for simpler operations, with static routes and manual failover via runbooks
Two Interconnects in separate metros, each on its own Cloud Router with BGP MED to prefer the lower-latency path, and HA VPN as warm backup
Provision a single 100 Gbps Dedicated Interconnect attachment in the primary metro and rely on Google's network to absorb local incidents
Use Cloud NAT plus an external IP and route hybrid traffic across the public internet with QoS marking on the on-prem edge devices
explanation: The 99.99% Dedicated Interconnect topology requires links in two separate metros, each on its own Cloud Router, with BGP for sub-minute convergence and MED to express path preference. HA VPN as warm backup is a recommended belt-and-suspenders pattern. Same-metro defeats the SLA; single link has no redundancy; Cloud NAT is for outbound public traffic.

text: Which Google Cloud load balancer terminates TLS at the edge, supports global anycast IP, integrates Cloud Armor, and routes HTTP(S) traffic to backends in multiple regions?
options: Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer
Global external Application Load Balancer
AWS Application Load Balancer
Regional Network Passthrough Load Balancer
explanation: Global external ALB is the only GCP option that combines anycast IP, edge TLS termination, multi-region routing, and Cloud Armor integration. Internal LBs don't serve external traffic; the AWS option is not GCP; regional passthrough NLBs are L4 single-region.

text: A globally accessible HTTP application requires SSL termination, path-based routing, integration with Cloud CDN, and protection by Cloud Armor with WAF rules. Which load balancer should you select?
options: Regional external Network Load Balancer (passthrough)
Global external Application Load Balancer
Regional internal Application Load Balancer
Cross-region internal Application Load Balancer
explanation: Only the global external Application Load Balancer integrates with Cloud CDN and supports global anycast IPs, path-based URL maps, SSL termination, and Cloud Armor WAF.

text: A global e-commerce platform runs Cloud Run services in three regions. Requirements: (1) a single anycast IPv4 published in DNS; (2) automatic failover away from an unhealthy region; (3) Cloud CDN for static assets at /assets/*; (4) path-based routing so /api/* hits the application backend; (5) cookie-based session affinity on /api/*. Which architecture BEST satisfies all five?
options: GeoDNS with three regional external Application Load Balancers, each with its own external IP and per-region Cloud CDN attached
Global external ALB: URL map sends `/assets/*` to a CDN backend bucket and `/api/*` to multi-region serverless NEGs with cookie affinity
Regional internal Application Load Balancers behind a public Cloud DNS record using weighted round-robin DNS for cross-region failover
Global external TCP Proxy Load Balancer with serverless NEGs, Cloud CDN, and a URL map providing path-based routing for `/api/*`
explanation: Only the global external ALB delivers a single anycast IP, multi-region serverless NEGs with automatic regional failover, URL-map-based path routing, Cloud CDN integration on a backend bucket, and cookie-based session affinity on a backend service. GeoDNS requires multiple IPs and DNS-cache-bound failover. Internal ALBs cannot serve public traffic. TCP Proxy LB is L4 only — no URL maps, no Cloud CDN.

text: A data-processing batch job runs nightly for 4 hours, is fault-tolerant, can resume from checkpoints, and you want to minimize compute cost. What configuration should you choose for the worker fleet?
options: On-demand N2 standard VMs in a regional MIG
Spot VMs in a regional MIG, with checkpointing to GCS
Cloud Functions invocations chained with Workflows
Reserved 3-year committed-use VMs
explanation: Spot VMs are typically 60-91% cheaper than on-demand and ideal for fault-tolerant batch with checkpoints.

text: A weekly ML training job runs 12–18 hours on GPU VMs and checkpoints to GCS every 30 minutes. The team wants the lowest cost while accepting that a preemption may cost up to one checkpoint interval of recomputation. Which choice is best?
options: On-demand A2 GPU VMs in a regional MIG
Spot GPU VMs with a startup script that resumes from the latest GCS checkpoint
1-year committed-use discount on enough A2 capacity to run 24/7
Cloud Run jobs (because they autoscale to zero)
explanation: Spot GPUs are 60–91% cheaper and the checkpointing already bounds the cost of a preemption. A 1-year CUD pays for full-time capacity even though the job uses ~6% of the week. Cloud Run jobs have a 60-minute task limit, far short of 12–18 hours.

text: A latency-sensitive trading service must run with: (1) p99 < 5 ms intra-rack latency; (2) consistent CPU performance (no noisy neighbors); (3) hardware-level isolation for regulatory reasons; (4) ability to bring custom licensed software bound to specific physical CPUs. Which compute option is BEST?
options: Cloud Run with min-instances, 2nd-gen execution, and CPU always-allocated for predictable per-request latency
GKE Autopilot with guaranteed-QoS pods and a node-affinity rule that pins them to dedicated machine families
Compute Engine sole-tenant nodes with a compact placement policy and per-VM licensing
Spot VMs in a regional MIG with compact placement and local SSD scratch storage on each instance
explanation: Sole-tenant nodes give physical-host isolation, compact placement keeps VMs in the same rack for sub-5ms latency, and per-VM licensing is supported. Cloud Run can't guarantee hardware isolation or compact placement. Autopilot abstracts nodes away. Spot VMs are preemptible — wrong workload class.

text: Compliance requires retaining contract PDFs for 7 years. They are read at most once per year (audits) and write-once. Which Cloud Storage class minimizes cost?
options: Standard
Nearline
Coldline
Archive
explanation: Archive is designed for data accessed less than once per year and offers the lowest storage price; minimum storage duration (365 days) easily fits a 7-year retention.

text: An application stores millions of small thumbnails. Reads are heavy in the first 30 days, then drop to <1/month, but objects must remain instantly available for 5 years. Which configuration minimizes lifetime cost?
options: Keep every thumbnail in Standard storage class for the entire 5-year retention period regardless of age or observed access pattern
Standard for 30 days; lifecycle rule transitioning to Nearline at 30 days, Coldline at 90, Archive at 365
Provision Archive storage class from day 1 to maximize at-rest savings and accept the per-access retrieval cost on every read
Move objects to Cloud Bigtable as binary cell values after 30 days and serve reads from Bigtable for the remaining 5-year window
explanation: Object Lifecycle Management age-based class transitions match the access-frequency curve. Archive from day 1 incurs heavy retrieval cost during the hot first 30 days. Bigtable is a NoSQL DB, not blob storage. Standard forever is the most expensive long-tail option.

text: A latency-sensitive analytics workload reads ~10 TB/day of immutable Parquet files written by an ingest pipeline. The team queries with Spark on Dataproc Serverless from one region, but other teams in different regions also need occasional reads. They want low cost and predictable latency for the primary region while still allowing cross-region access without manually copying data. Which storage configuration fits best?
options: Multi-region Standard bucket co-located with the primary region
Dual-region Standard bucket with the primary region as one of the two regions, using turbo replication
Regional Coldline bucket in the primary region only
Regional Standard bucket in the primary region; teams in other regions transfer copies via Storage Transfer Service
explanation: Dual-region buckets give regional-class performance from each of the two regions, optional turbo replication for tight RPO, and a single bucket all teams can read from.

text: Which Google Cloud IAM principle should be the default when granting access to a service account?
options: Grant the basic Owner role on the project so the workload has access to anything it might need today or in the future
Grant only the predefined or custom role(s) the workload needs (least privilege)
Grant roles/editor to every workload service account so all teams use the same standard role across every environment
Grant the AWS Administrator IAM policy that mirrors the workload's existing entitlements in the source cloud account
explanation: Least privilege is the IAM foundation: bind only the roles required for the workload. Owner/Editor grant far more than needed; the AWS option is not a GCP concept.

text: Your security team requires that storage buckets containing customer PII can only be accessed by workloads inside specific VPC networks, even if a leaked service-account key were used externally. Which control enforces this?
options: Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) on the buckets
IAM Conditions restricting access by request.time
VPC Service Controls perimeter that includes the storage project
Uniform bucket-level access with public access prevention
explanation: VPC Service Controls create a network-aware service perimeter: API calls to protected services from outside the perimeter are rejected even with valid credentials.

text: A workload running on AWS EC2 needs to read objects from a Google Cloud Storage bucket. The security team forbids creating long-lived service-account keys. What is the recommended approach?
options: Generate a JSON service account key, store it in AWS Secrets Manager, mount it on EC2, and rotate it weekly with a Lambda function
Configure Workload Identity Federation with AWS as the IdP so the EC2 IAM role can impersonate a Google Cloud service account
Use a long-lived service account key delivered via EC2 user-data, restricted by IAM Conditions and bucket-level public access prevention
Generate short-lived OAuth 2.0 user credentials via gcloud auth application-default login and cache them in EC2 instance metadata
explanation: Workload Identity Federation lets external identities (AWS IAM role, in this case) exchange their native tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials, removing the need for any long-lived SA key. Option A still produces a long-lived key (rotation weekly is still 'long-lived' by security standards). Option C uses a long-lived key — IAM Conditions don't change its lifetime. Option D requires interactive user credentials, which don't exist on a server.

text: You want to restrict all projects in your organization so that Compute Engine VMs cannot be created with external IP addresses. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: A custom Cloud Asset Inventory feed plus a Cloud Function that detects new VMs with external IPs and deletes the NIC's access config
An organization policy with constraint constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny all, applied at the org with allowlist exceptions per folder
An IAM deny policy at the org node that denies compute.instances.setMachineType to all principals
A VPC Service Controls perimeter around the Compute Engine API plus an org-wide hierarchical firewall denying all egress to 0.0.0.0/0
explanation: constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess is the purpose-built org policy constraint that *prevents* external IPs at create time, enforceable at org/folder/project with allowlists. Detect-and-remediate (option A) leaves a window where the IP exists. Option C denies the wrong permission and breaks legitimate use. VPC-SC + egress-deny (D) doesn't stop external IP attachment and breaks all outbound traffic.

text: A regulated customer requires that the encryption keys protecting their Cloud Storage data NEVER reside inside Google's infrastructure, and that Google must call out to the customer's key system for every cryptographic operation — so that revoking access at the customer side immediately blocks Google from decrypting. Which option meets this?
options: Google-managed encryption keys (default), with Access Approval enabled for any Google personnel access
CMEK with keys in Cloud KMS at the software protection level, with automatic 30-day key rotation enabled
CMEK with Cloud HSM-backed keys in a customer-controlled key ring inside Google's KMS infrastructure
CMEK with Cloud External Key Manager referencing keys held in the customer's external KMS/HSM
explanation: Cloud EKM keeps the key material in the customer's external KMS/HSM; Google must reach out for every wrap/unwrap, and revocation at the external KMS immediately denies Google. CMEK (software or HSM) still stores the key inside Google. Default Google-managed keys are entirely Google-controlled.

text: A healthcare provider is deploying an EHR application on Google Cloud and must comply with HIPAA. Which combination provides the strongest defense-in-depth?
options: Sign a BAA, restrict to HIPAA-eligible services, and enable Data Access audit logs, CMEK, and VPC Service Controls
Sign a BAA, deploy on Compute Engine VMs, and rely on default Google-managed encryption at rest for all PHI
Skip the BAA, store PHI in a private Cloud Storage bucket with uniform bucket-level access and signed URLs only
Sign a BAA, use HIPAA-eligible services, and rely on long random Cloud Storage object names plus public-access prevention
explanation: HIPAA on GCP requires a signed BAA, use of HIPAA-eligible services, and best practices include enabling Data Access audit logs, CMEK, and VPC-SC.

text: A team wants to detect misconfigurations (publicly exposed buckets, over-privileged service accounts, open firewall rules) and high-severity vulnerabilities across all projects, with findings centralized for the security team. Which Google Cloud product is the right hub?
options: Cloud Asset Inventory with scheduled exports to a BigQuery dataset and custom SQL detectors written by the security team
Security Command Center with Security Health Analytics and Web Security Scanner enabled
Cloud Logging with org-level log-based metrics and a custom alerting policy per misconfiguration class
Cloud Armor security policies plus Recommender API recommendations triaged in a per-project ticket queue
explanation: Security Command Center is the centralized security and risk management hub; Security Health Analytics is the built-in detector for the listed misconfigurations and Web Security Scanner finds web vulns. Asset Inventory is raw resource state — you'd be rebuilding SCC from scratch. Log-based metrics can't see most of the listed posture issues. Cloud Armor protects HTTP backends, not posture.

text: A US healthcare org processing PHI on GCP must meet: (1) signed BAA; (2) no data egress to non-HIPAA-eligible services; (3) all admin actions audited including reads; (4) network-isolated PHI services; (5) quarterly attestation of effective controls with low manual effort. Which combination BEST satisfies all five?
options: BAA + `restrictServiceUsage` org policy + Data Access audit logs + VPC-SC perimeter on PHI projects + SCC Premium continuous monitoring
Sign a BAA, deploy in a single shared project with CMEK and Google-managed keys, and run weekly manual access reviews
Skip the BAA but enable CMEK with Cloud EKM, VPC-SC, Access Approval, and quarterly third-party penetration testing
Sign a BAA, deploy across HIPAA-eligible services, and rely on long random Cloud Storage object names with signed URLs
explanation: All five controls map directly: BAA (legal), `restrictServiceUsage` enforces eligible-only services, Data Access logs cover read-side auditing, VPC-SC isolates PHI from data exfiltration, and SCC Premium provides continuous-compliance evidence for attestation. The other options each violate at least one HIPAA-on-GCP requirement.

text: You want secrets (DB passwords, API keys) to be centrally stored, automatically rotated where supported, version-controlled, and accessible to Cloud Run via IAM-controlled environment variables. What's the right service?
options: Cloud KMS
Secret Manager
GCS bucket with object-level IAM
Cloud Asset Inventory
explanation: Secret Manager is purpose-built for secrets: versioning, IAM, rotation, automatic mounting into Cloud Run as env vars or files.

text: You need to rotate a database password stored in Secret Manager every 30 days, with no human in the loop. Which design is idiomatic on GCP?
options: Enable Cloud KMS automatic rotation on the key encrypting the secret; configure the rotation period to 30 days so the underlying ciphertext is re-wrapped
Configure Secret Manager rotation: a Pub/Sub topic on a 30-day schedule triggers a Cloud Function that mints a new password, updates the database, and adds a new secret version
Schedule a Cloud Scheduler job calling a custom Cloud Run service that overwrites the same secret version in place every 30 days using addSecretVersion with deletion of older versions
Configure a Cloud Composer DAG that runs monthly, generates a new password using a Python operator, writes it to a versioned object in Cloud Storage, and updates the DB password from there
explanation: Secret Manager's built-in rotation publishes to a Pub/Sub topic on the configured cadence; the subscriber (typically a Cloud Function) rotates the credential at the source DB and adds a new secret version. KMS key rotation rotates the wrapping key, not the secret value (the password is unchanged). Overwriting versions in place defeats versioning and rollback. A Composer DAG dropping passwords in GCS objects bypasses Secret Manager entirely and is a security regression.

text: A Cloud Run service stores a PostgreSQL connection string in Secret Manager. The team wants: (1) version pinning (so a bad rotation can be rolled back instantly); (2) the secret never written to disk in the container; (3) automatic propagation to a new revision when the secret version changes; (4) IAM-scoped per environment. Which configuration BEST meets all four?
options: Pin a specific secret version (e.g. `versions/3`) as an env var; grant `secretAccessor` to per-env service accounts; redeploy via Cloud Deploy on version bumps
Bake the connection string into the container image and rebuild for each rotation, using image signing for tamper protection
Mount `secrets/db/versions/latest` as a file at startup; share one Cloud Run service account across dev, staging, and prod for simplicity
Store the connection string in a Cloud Storage object with uniform bucket-level access and fetch it at container startup with curl
explanation: Pinning a specific version enables instant rollback (just redeploy with the prior version); env-var injection avoids on-disk writes; per-env service accounts give IAM scoping; pipeline-triggered redeploys propagate version bumps. `:latest` defeats pinning; baked-in or public storage are obvious anti-patterns.

text: Your analyst team wants to run nightly SQL transformations on data already in BigQuery, with no servers, no orchestrator, and the simplest possible setup. Which feature fits?
options: Dataproc cluster running Spark SQL
BigQuery scheduled queries
Dataflow batch pipeline
Cloud Composer DAG with a BashOperator
explanation: Scheduled queries run a SQL statement on a cron schedule directly inside BigQuery — no infrastructure to manage. Dataproc/Dataflow/Composer are all heavier than required for in-warehouse SQL transforms.

text: You need to ingest streaming clickstream events at ~1M events/sec, run windowed aggregations and fraud-detection joins in flight, and land both raw and enriched events in BigQuery. What's the recommended GCP-native pipeline?
options: Pub/Sub → Dataflow streaming (Apache Beam) → BigQuery
Cloud Storage → Dataproc batch → BigQuery
Cloud Functions → BigQuery streaming inserts directly
Pub/Sub → Cloud Run with manual sharding → BigQuery
explanation: Pub/Sub + Dataflow is the canonical streaming ETL pattern on GCP. Dataflow handles windowing, watermarks, and exactly-once semantics into BigQuery.

text: A streaming pipeline ingests 250k events/sec from Pub/Sub. Requirements: (1) exactly-once writes to BigQuery; (2) 5-minute tumbling windows joined with a slowly-changing dimension table in Cloud Spanner; (3) handle late events up to 30 minutes via watermarks; (4) backfills must reuse the same code as streaming. Which design is BEST?
options: Cloud Functions per event writing via BigQuery streaming inserts; nightly Dataflow batch reconciliation handles late events and dedup
Dataflow Beam streaming with Storage Write API EXACTLY_ONCE; Spanner side-input; 30m allowed lateness; same code reused for batch backfills
Dataproc Spark Structured Streaming with at-least-once writes; periodic dedup queries in BigQuery; Hive-partitioned backfill jobs
Cloud Run with manual sharding by message attribute and per-shard BigQuery streaming inserts; Cloud Scheduler reruns shards for backfills
explanation: Beam unifies streaming and batch (same code for backfill); BigQuery Storage Write API supports exactly-once; allowed lateness handles the 30-min watermark; Spanner side-input provides the dimension join. Cloud Functions/Cloud Run lack windowing/watermarks; at-least-once Spark requires post-hoc dedup.

text: Which Google Cloud service provides serverless, petabyte-scale SQL analytics with a separation of compute (slots) and storage?
options: Bigtable
Cloud SQL
BigQuery
Amazon Redshift
explanation: BigQuery is the serverless analytics engine with separate compute (slots) and columnar storage. Bigtable is a wide-column NoSQL store; Cloud SQL is a transactional RDBMS; Redshift is AWS.

text: Your analytics team wants ad-hoc SQL over petabytes with no infrastructure to manage, plus the ability to query data in Cloud Storage without ingesting it. Which feature pair fits?
options: BigQuery + BigLake external tables
Cloud SQL + read replicas
Bigtable + Dataflow
Spanner + federated queries
explanation: BigQuery is the serverless analytics engine; BigLake external tables expose Parquet/ORC/Avro in GCS as governed BigQuery tables without ingestion.

text: A 50 TB BigQuery sales fact table is queried hundreds of times per day. Almost every query filters by sale_date (last 90 days) and groups/filters by region. Costs are spiking and p95 latency is climbing. Which combination most directly addresses BOTH problems?
options: Add a covering BigQuery materialized view per analyst
Partition by sale_date and cluster by region; purchase a slot reservation (BI Engine + edition) to cap and predict cost
Move the table to Bigtable for faster scans
Export the table to Parquet in GCS and query via BigLake only
explanation: Date partitioning prunes 90 days vs. full-scan; clustering on region further prunes within partitions — directly cutting bytes scanned and latency. Slot reservations make spend predictable. Per-analyst materialized views proliferate cost. Bigtable is a NoSQL key-value store, not a SQL analytics engine.

text: Your team wants a managed API platform supporting versioning, OAuth/JWT enforcement, rate limiting per developer, monetization, and a developer portal. Which Google Cloud service is the right fit?
options: API Gateway
Apigee
Cloud Endpoints with ESPv2
Cloud Load Balancing with URL maps
explanation: Apigee is the full-lifecycle API management platform: versioning, advanced policies, monetization, developer portals.

text: A platform team is choosing between Apigee X, API Gateway, and Cloud Endpoints (ESPv2). Requirements: (1) monetization with rate plans; (2) developer portal with self-service onboarding; (3) advanced policies (mediation, transformation, OAuth2 + JWT); (4) hybrid deployment to also front on-prem APIs; (5) lowest ops effort. Which is BEST?
options: API Gateway — the GCP-managed gateway
Cloud Endpoints (ESPv2) running in GKE
Apigee X (managed, multi-tenant)
Build a custom NGINX cluster
explanation: Only Apigee X provides monetization, a developer portal, advanced policy chains, and Apigee Hybrid for fronting on-prem APIs — all fully managed. API Gateway and Endpoints are simpler and cheaper but lack monetization, portal, and hybrid; NGINX is fully self-managed.

text: Which Google Cloud product is the simplest fit for an event-driven function that runs in response to a new file landing in Cloud Storage?
options: A Compute Engine VM running a cron job every minute that lists the bucket via the API and processes any newly added objects
Cloud Run function (Cloud Functions) triggered by the GCS finalize event
A GKE DaemonSet on every node that polls the bucket on a fixed interval and dispatches work to in-cluster worker pods
AWS Lambda with an S3 event trigger reading from a mirrored S3 bucket synchronized from the Cloud Storage source bucket
explanation: Cloud Functions / Cloud Run functions natively support GCS finalize event triggers via Eventarc — no polling, no cluster, no servers. The other options either poll or are non-GCP.

text: You're modernizing a monolith into microservices on GKE. Internal services need L7 features (gRPC, retries, circuit breaking) and mTLS between pods, with no external exposure. Which solution best fits?
options: Global external Application Load Balancer in front of every microservice
Cloud Service Mesh (managed Istio) providing mTLS, retries, and L7 traffic management between services
Regional external Network Load Balancer per service
Cloud NAT with custom routing
explanation: Cloud Service Mesh delivers pod-to-pod mTLS and L7 traffic management (gRPC, retries, circuit breakers) without exposing services externally. External ALBs/NLBs would expose internal services and don't provide mTLS between pods.

text: You're modernizing a monolithic Java app. The team wants to break it into services incrementally. Which 'strangler fig' pattern works on GCP with minimal new infrastructure for routing?
options: Put a global external Application Load Balancer in front; create URL map rules that route specific paths to new Cloud Run services while the rest still points to the legacy backend
Rewrite everything before deploying any of it
Deploy both versions to different VPCs with no shared ingress
Use DNS round-robin between old and new
explanation: URL-map-based routing on the global ALB is the classic strangler-fig enabler: peel off endpoints one path at a time without changing client URLs.

text: Per the Google Cloud Architecture Framework's operational excellence pillar, which practice should be foundational from day one of a new service?
options: Add observability (logs, metrics, traces, SLOs) so the team can detect and diagnose issues — instrument before launch
Optimize cost above all else; observability can be added later
Deploy manually to production until traffic patterns stabilize
Use only Google-managed services so observability is unnecessary
explanation: The framework emphasizes 'observe everything' as a day-one operational excellence principle.

text: A team wants to track an availability SLO for a Cloud Run API and be alerted when error budget is being burned aggressively, while suppressing noise from short blips. Which approach should they implement?
options: Add a metric-threshold alerting policy on the Cloud Run 5xx-rate metric with a 5-minute alignment window and the default trigger condition of 'any time series violates'
Define a request-based SLO in Cloud Monitoring and add a multi-burn-rate policy (fast-burn 14.4× over 1h AND slow-burn 6× over 6h)
Configure a single low-burn-rate (1×) alerting condition over a 24-hour window so the team is paged once daily summarizing the day's error budget consumption
Build a custom Cloud Function on a Cloud Scheduler trigger that queries the Monitoring API every hour and pages on-call if 5xx > 100 in the prior hour
explanation: Cloud Monitoring SLOs natively compute burn rate; the multi-window/multi-burn-rate pattern from the SRE Workbook catches severe incidents fast while suppressing blips. A static 5xx threshold has no SLO context — it pages on every spike. A 1× single-window policy detects burns far too late (and a daily summary isn't an alert). A custom Cloud Function reinvents the SLO machinery poorly.

text: A team is designing observability for a new Cloud Run service. They want: (1) request-based SLOs in Cloud Monitoring; (2) structured JSON logs correlated to traces by trace ID; (3) one dashboard combining latency, error rate, saturation; (4) alerts wired to PagerDuty AND a chat channel; (5) a runbook URL surfaced on every page. Which approach BEST satisfies all five?
options: Plaintext stdout logs, default Cloud Monitoring service dashboards, and a single shared email notification channel for every alerting policy
Cloud Monitoring SLO + multi-burn-rate policy; structured JSON logs with `logging.googleapis.com/trace`; custom dashboard; PagerDuty + chat channels; `documentation.content` runbook
Skip SLOs and rely on uptime checks plus a single 5xx-rate alert paging the team email when error rate exceeds 1% for five minutes
Use Cloud Profiler as the alerting backend; a per-service Cloud Function forwards profile-based incidents to PagerDuty and a chat channel
explanation: Each control maps to a specific Cloud Monitoring/Logging feature: SLO API, trace-field correlation, dashboard widgets, multiple notification channels per policy, and `documentation.content` for runbook links. The other options each miss multiple requirements.

text: Per Google's Architecture Framework, which is the preferred ordering when an incident is in progress?
options: Diagnose → Mitigate → Communicate
Mitigate → Communicate → Diagnose
Communicate → Diagnose → Mitigate
Wait → Escalate → Diagnose
explanation: SRE practice is mitigate first (stop user pain), keep stakeholders informed, then diagnose. This is also the framework's recommended incident-response sequencing.

text: Per Architecture Framework operational excellence, which practice should be in place BEFORE a service handles production traffic?
options: Defined SLIs/SLOs, runbooks for the top failure modes, and a tested rollback path
A public status page wired to the on-call rotation, with no formal SLOs, runbooks, or rollback procedure documented yet
A blank postmortem template stored in the team wiki, to be filled in if and when the first production incident occurs
A policy that every production deploy be reviewed and approved by an architect manually each time a change is shipped
explanation: The framework's pre-prod readiness checklist centers on measurable SLIs/SLOs, runbooks, and rollback. Templates and manual reviews don't substitute for instrumentation and reversibility.

text: A fintech runs three workload classes on GCP: (1) an order-processing API on GKE that runs 24/7 and cannot be interrupted; (2) a nightly ML training job that checkpoints every 15 minutes and tolerates restarts; (3) on-demand analytics export jobs lasting <30 minutes that are fully fault-tolerant. Which cost strategy BEST matches each workload independently?
options: 1-year CUD for all three workloads
1-year CUD for the order API; Spot VMs for the ML training job; Spot VMs for the export jobs
Spot VMs for all three workloads
On-demand for all three; rely on sustained-use discounts only
explanation: CUDs reward 24/7 steady-state usage and protect the non-interruptible API. Spot is ideal for both fault-tolerant workloads (ML with checkpoints, short-lived exports). Putting Spot under the order API risks outages; CUDs for spiky/short jobs strands commitment; SUD-only leaves money on the table for the steady workload.

text: An auditor asks where to find a single, signed report describing the scope and findings of Google Cloud's most recent SOC 2 Type II audit so the company can attach it to its own compliance package. What should the architect direct them to?
options: The Google Cloud Status Dashboard
Compliance Reports Manager in the Trust and Security center
Cloud Audit Logs exported to BigQuery
The customer's Access Transparency log bucket
explanation: Compliance Reports Manager is where Google publishes signed third-party audit reports (SOC, ISO, PCI, etc.) for customers to download. The status dashboard reports outages, Audit Logs record API calls, and Access Transparency records Google-staff access — none deliver third-party attestations.

text: A team needs to back up Compute Engine persistent disks daily and retain copies in another region with no manual scripting. Which built-in feature satisfies this?
options: Cloud Storage Transfer Service jobs reading the disks
A snapshot schedule attached to the disks with a multi-region storage location
Filestore backups copied with rsync
Persistent Disk Async Replication only
explanation: Snapshot schedules are the managed daily-backup mechanism for PDs and accept a multi-region storage location for cross-region durability. Storage Transfer Service does not back up PDs directly, Filestore is a different service, and PD Async Replication is for low-RPO DR (block-level continuous replication) rather than scheduled backups with retention.

text: A US healthcare ISV wants to offer its SaaS to EU customers, who require that PHI never leave the EU and that Google personnel access requests be visible to the customer. Which two-control combination is the MOST direct fit?
options: Cloud KMS with software-protected keys plus IAM Conditions
Assured Workloads for EU regions plus Access Transparency with Access Approval
VPC Service Controls plus Cloud Logging exports to a US bucket
Organization Policy `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` plus default at-rest encryption
explanation: Assured Workloads enforces EU data and personnel residency for the workload, and Access Transparency + Access Approval surface and gate Google-staff access. KMS+IAM Conditions and Org Policy do not enforce data-residency boundaries on their own, and exporting logs to a US bucket would itself violate the EU-only requirement.

text: A retail platform must continue serving read traffic during a single-zone outage but can tolerate a brief read-only window for writes. Cost matters. Which Cloud SQL configuration BEST meets the requirement?
options: Single-zone instance with on-demand backups
Regional (HA) Cloud SQL instance with automatic failover and a read replica in the same region
Two single-zone primaries replicating to each other with custom failover scripting
Cross-region read replica only, with manual promotion
explanation: A regional (HA) Cloud SQL instance synchronously replicates to a standby in another zone and fails over automatically, satisfying the zonal-outage requirement; an in-region read replica continues serving reads through the failover window. Self-managed dual primaries are unsupported and a cross-region replica alone has higher RPO and requires manual promotion.

text: A global SaaS provider needs an active/active relational store with strong consistency, RPO of zero across regions, and the ability to survive a regional failure with no data loss. Latency-sensitive writes are tolerated up to ~100 ms. Which configuration BEST fits?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL HA in one region with cross-region read replicas
Spanner multi-region instance configuration (e.g. nam-eur-asia1)
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL with cross-region replicas and manual failover
Bigtable replicated cluster with multi-cluster routing
explanation: Spanner multi-region configurations provide synchronous, strongly-consistent replication across regions with RPO of zero and survive a regional outage transparently — the only option that meets active/active with strong consistency. Cloud SQL and AlloyDB cross-region replication is asynchronous (RPO > 0); Bigtable has eventual consistency and lacks SQL transactions across rows.

text: A regulated insurer needs an immutable backup copy of GCS objects that cannot be deleted by an attacker who compromises an admin account, with a 7-year retention obligation. Which design satisfies the requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Object Versioning enabled on the source bucket only
A separate target bucket with a Bucket Lock retention policy of 7 years and Object Versioning, populated by Storage Transfer Service
Daily exports to a Coldline bucket with IAM Conditions limiting deletes to one principal
Customer-managed encryption keys with rotation disabled
explanation: Bucket Lock makes a retention policy permanent: no principal — not even a project owner — can delete objects before the retention period expires, providing true immutability. Versioning alone, IAM Conditions, and CMEK rotation policies can all be undone by a sufficiently privileged compromised account.

text: A team wants to deploy a stateless container that scales to zero between requests and bills only while requests are in flight. Which compute option fits BEST?
options: Compute Engine MIG with min size 1
Cloud Run service
GKE Standard with a system pool of size 3
App Engine flexible environment
explanation: Cloud Run scales containers from zero to N based on request load and bills request-driven CPU. A MIG with min=1 always runs at least one VM, GKE Standard requires running nodes, and App Engine flex keeps a minimum instance running.

text: Which Google Cloud service is the recommended starting point for a new event-driven function that runs in response to a Pub/Sub message and finishes in under a minute?
options: Cloud Run functions (2nd gen)
GKE Autopilot deployment
Compute Engine startup script
Dataflow streaming job
explanation: Cloud Run functions (the rebranded Cloud Functions, 2nd gen, built on Cloud Run) are purpose-built for short-lived event-driven workloads triggered by Pub/Sub. GKE and Compute Engine require always-on infrastructure; Dataflow is for continuous data processing pipelines, not single-message handlers.

text: A team is choosing between GKE Autopilot and GKE Standard for a new service. They want minimal node management, predictable per-pod billing, and want Google to enforce security best practices on the nodes. Which is the better fit and why?
options: GKE Standard, because Autopilot does not support Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
GKE Autopilot, because Google manages and hardens the nodes and you pay per pod resource request
GKE Standard, because Autopilot lacks regional clusters
GKE Autopilot, because it lets you SSH into nodes for custom kernel tuning
explanation: Autopilot manages nodes for you, applies hardened defaults, and bills based on pod resource requests, matching the stated goals. HPA works on both modes; Autopilot supports regional clusters; node SSH is explicitly disallowed in Autopilot — that's the tradeoff, not a feature.

text: A Java microservice needs persistent SSH-less HTTP/2 traffic, 30-minute request timeouts, GPU acceleration on a fraction of requests, and the ability to scale to zero. Which compute platform satisfies ALL these requirements?
options: App Engine standard environment
Cloud Run service with GPU enabled
Cloud Run functions (2nd gen)
GKE Standard with Spot pools
explanation: Cloud Run services support HTTP/2, request timeouts up to 60 minutes, attached GPUs, and scale-to-zero. App Engine standard caps timeouts and lacks GPU; Cloud Run functions inherit Cloud Run features but are oriented toward event/HTTP triggers without persistent service config; GKE Standard does not scale to zero.

text: A trading firm needs sub-200-microsecond P99 between two Compute Engine VMs for an order-matching algorithm. They also need MPI-style collective operations across 32 nodes for nightly risk simulations. Which compute strategy is BEST?
options: C3 VMs in different regions connected via Cloud VPN
Compact placement policy with C3 (or H3) VMs in the same zone for the matching pair, and an HPC-tuned cluster (Slurm-on-GCP) using H3 with placement policy for risk sims
GKE Autopilot with default scheduling for both workloads
E2 shared-core VMs with sole-tenant nodes
explanation: Compact placement policy with C3/H3 VMs in one zone minimizes inter-VM latency; H3 (HPC) with placement policy is Google's recommended path for tightly-coupled MPI workloads. VPN crossing regions destroys latency, GKE Autopilot is too abstract for this control, and E2 is not designed for low-latency or HPC.

text: A company is migrating a Windows-based commercial ERP that requires a specific Windows Server version, joins the corporate AD domain, and uses license-bound binaries. They want to lift-and-shift quickly with minimal refactoring. Which choice is MOST appropriate?
options: Re-platform onto Cloud Run for Windows containers
Compute Engine Windows VMs in a sole-tenant node group with bring-your-own-license, joined to AD via Managed Microsoft AD
GKE Windows Server node pools running the ERP as a container
App Engine standard with the .NET runtime
explanation: Compute Engine VMs (sole-tenant, BYOL) is the canonical lift-and-shift for licensed Windows ERP, and Managed Microsoft AD provides the AD integration without running domain controllers. Cloud Run does not run Windows containers, packaging a full Windows ERP into GKE containers is a heavy refactor, and App Engine standard cannot host a stateful licensed ERP.

text: Which storage class is the cheapest for objects accessed at most once per year and can be used as part of a lifecycle policy from Standard?
options: Nearline
Coldline
Archive
Multi-Regional Standard
explanation: Archive storage has the lowest at-rest price and a 365-day minimum storage duration, intended for once-a-year or rarer access. Nearline (30 days) and Coldline (90 days) target more frequent access; Multi-Regional Standard is the most expensive tier.

text: Two GCE instances in different VPCs in the same project need private IP connectivity. Which is the simplest supported design?
options: Set up Cloud VPN between the two VPCs
Use VPC Network Peering between the two VPCs
Place a NAT VM in each VPC and route through it
Move both VMs into the same subnet manually
explanation: VPC Network Peering connects two VPCs with private IP routing, no gateways, no transit hops. Cloud VPN is unnecessary overhead for same-project peering; NAT bridging is non-standard; subnets cannot span VPCs.

text: An analytics team needs petabyte-scale, columnar, serverless SQL with separation of storage and compute and per-query pricing. Which service is the BEST fit?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with the columnar extension
BigQuery
Bigtable with SQL via Data Boost
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL
explanation: BigQuery is Google's serverless petabyte-scale columnar warehouse with decoupled storage/compute and on-demand or capacity-based billing. Cloud SQL and AlloyDB are OLTP row stores; Bigtable is a wide-column NoSQL store, not a SQL warehouse.

text: An on-prem application uses a POSIX NFS share to read shared media assets across many compute hosts. The team wants to migrate to GCP with the LEAST application change. Which storage option is appropriate?
options: Cloud Storage FUSE on every VM, using a single bucket
Filestore Enterprise tier, mounted via NFSv3 from each VM
Local SSDs replicated with rsync
Persistent Disk in Read-Many mode mounted on all VMs
explanation: Filestore is a managed NFS service designed for POSIX-shared workloads, requiring no application changes. GCS FUSE is not POSIX-compliant and has performance limitations for shared writes; rsync between local SSDs is not shared storage; PD multi-writer is read-only and limited to two VMs.

text: A global mobile gaming backend needs single-digit-millisecond reads, automatic horizontal scaling to millions of QPS, and multi-region replication, with a key-value access pattern (no joins). Which database fits BEST?
options: Spanner multi-region configuration, sized to absorb the millions of QPS using strongly consistent global reads and writes
Bigtable with replicated clusters in two regions and multi-cluster routing
Memorystore for Redis Cluster sharded across many nodes in a single region with persistence enabled and read replicas
Firestore in Datastore mode, single region
explanation: Bigtable handles massive QPS, low single-digit-ms reads, and supports replicated clusters across regions with eventually-consistent multi-cluster routing — ideal for high-throughput key-value gaming workloads. Spanner is overkill (and costlier) for pure key-value, Memorystore is in-memory and not durable at this scale, and single-region Firestore fails the multi-region requirement.

text: A team needs an internal HTTP load balancer that distributes traffic across services in multiple regions with cross-region failover and a single anycast VIP, while remaining inside the VPC (no public IP). Which load balancer should they choose?
options: Internal regional Application Load Balancer in each region with DNS round-robin
Cross-region internal Application Load Balancer
External Application Load Balancer with private IP
Internal passthrough Network Load Balancer
explanation: The cross-region internal Application Load Balancer provides a single internal anycast VIP that routes across regional backends with built-in failover — exactly the requirement. Regional internal ALBs require DNS-based stitching, external ALBs cannot be private-only, and internal passthrough NLB is layer 4 and regional.

text: Which Google Cloud service helps inventory an on-premises VMware estate and recommend right-sized GCE shapes before a migration?
options: Migrate to Containers
Migration Center (with discovery client)
Database Migration Service
Transfer Appliance
explanation: Migration Center performs server/VM discovery and produces sizing and TCO assessments to plan a GCE migration. Migrate to Containers re-platforms VMs to GKE, DMS is for databases, and Transfer Appliance moves bulk storage — none does discovery & assessment.

text: A team plans to ship 200 TB of historical files from a remote data center with limited bandwidth to Cloud Storage. Which service is purpose-built for this?
options: Storage Transfer Service over the public internet
Transfer Appliance
gsutil parallel upload from a single workstation
Filestore replication
explanation: Transfer Appliance ships physical storage to the customer for offline data loads at multi-hundred-TB scale where network transfer would be too slow. STS over the internet is bandwidth-limited, gsutil from one workstation is even worse, and Filestore is an NFS service.

text: A company is migrating an on-prem Oracle database to PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL with minimal downtime. Which Google service supports the heterogeneous CDC migration?
options: Datastream + Database Migration Service
Storage Transfer Service
Pub/Sub with custom Dataflow
Cloud Functions polling Oracle every minute
explanation: Database Migration Service supports heterogeneous Oracle→PostgreSQL migrations using Datastream for change data capture, providing low-downtime cutover. STS is for files, custom Dataflow/Functions are heavy DIY paths that DMS+Datastream replace.

doc_links.title: Database Migration Service — Oracle to PostgreSQL

text: A retailer is performing a wave-based migration from on-prem VMware to GCE. They want to keep on-prem and cloud workloads on the same private network during the transition with minimal additional gateways. Which connectivity option is BEST?
options: Public IPs and IAP TCP forwarding
Cloud VPN HA tunnels for steady production-grade traffic
Dedicated Interconnect or Partner Interconnect
Cloud NAT with reverse routes
explanation: For sustained migration traffic with predictable bandwidth and low latency, Dedicated or Partner Interconnect provides private L2/L3 connectivity that scales beyond the per-tunnel limits of Cloud VPN. HA VPN works for smaller waves; IAP TCP forwarding and Cloud NAT do not provide on-prem private connectivity.

text: An enterprise is moving 800 VMware VMs running stateful Java apps with attached MS SQL databases. They want a phased move that converts a subset of VMs into containers while keeping the databases on managed services. Which combination is BEST?
options: Migrate to Virtual Machines for stateless app VMs to GCE; Migrate to Containers (M4C) for selected stateless tiers; Database Migration Service for SQL Server→Cloud SQL for SQL Server
Lift-and-shift everything to a single GKE Autopilot cluster
Rewrite all 800 apps in Cloud Run before migrating
Use Storage Transfer Service to copy VMDK files into GCS and run them from there
explanation: Migrate to VMs handles bulk lift-and-shift, M4C re-platforms candidate VMs to containers, and DMS handles the SQL Server side onto Cloud SQL — a realistic phased pattern. Forcing everything into GKE or Cloud Run is a rewrite, and GCS does not run VMDKs.

text: During cutover of a heterogeneous DMS migration from MySQL to Cloud SQL, the application must continue dual-write briefly while the team validates parity, then switch reads. Which migration strategy does this describe and what is the principal risk?
options: Big-bang migration; risk: rollback is impossible
Parallel run with dual-write; risk: write conflicts and divergence between source and target if not carefully sequenced
Canary deployment; risk: the canary cluster cannot handle DR
Strangler-fig refactor; risk: monolith cannot be decomposed
explanation: Dual-writing to source and target during validation is a parallel-run pattern; the canonical risk is divergence/conflict between the two stores if writes aren't carefully sequenced or arbitrated. Big-bang lacks the dual-write step, canary/strangler-fig describe service-level rollouts not data migrations.

text: Which service lets a team publish events from one producer and have multiple independent subscribers consume them at their own pace?
options: Cloud Tasks
Pub/Sub
Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Storage notifications only
explanation: Pub/Sub is Google's managed publish/subscribe service supporting multiple independent subscribers per topic with at-least-once delivery. Cloud Tasks targets per-task execution to a single worker, Cloud Scheduler triggers cron-style jobs, and GCS notifications are a producer that typically publishes into Pub/Sub.

text: Which feature on a Compute Engine managed instance group automatically adjusts the number of running VMs based on CPU utilization or load-balancer metrics?
options: Health checks
Autohealing
Autoscaling
Live migration
explanation: Autoscaling on a MIG adds or removes instances based on signals such as CPU utilization, LB serving capacity, or custom Monitoring metrics. Health checks identify unhealthy instances; autohealing recreates them; live migration is a maintenance feature, not a scaling control.

text: A team designs a write-heavy ingestion API behind Cloud Run that occasionally receives 50x normal traffic for short bursts. They want to absorb spikes without dropping events and decouple producers from consumers. Which pattern fits BEST?
options: Cloud Run handlers write each request synchronously into Bigtable, sized for steady-state QPS and relying on client retries during spikes
Cloud Run publishes to Pub/Sub; downstream Cloud Run/Functions subscribers process at their own rate with retries
Buffer all incoming events to a single Memorystore for Redis instance and process them inline before responding to the caller
Write each request as a row into a regional Cloud SQL instance and use Cloud Scheduler cron jobs to drain the table periodically
explanation: Pub/Sub absorbs bursts, decouples ingest from processing, and supports retries with dead-letter topics — the classic spike-absorbing pattern. Direct Bigtable writes couple producer rate to backend capacity, a single Memorystore is a SPOF and not durable, and Cloud SQL + cron forfeits low-latency event handling.

text: An e-commerce service stores session state in a single Memorystore for Redis instance and the team is worried about both single-zone failure and traffic growth. Which evolution is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Move sessions into Cloud Storage as JSON objects
Switch to Memorystore for Redis Cluster (or Memorystore for Valkey) with multiple shards and read replicas across zones
Switch to Bigtable single-cluster
Disable Redis and use sticky sessions on the load balancer only
explanation: Memorystore for Redis Cluster (and Valkey) shards data across nodes for horizontal scale and supports cross-zone replicas for HA — directly addressing both growth and zonal failure. GCS JSON is too slow for sessions, Bigtable single-cluster is single-zone, and sticky sessions alone don't survive instance loss.

text: A SaaS platform expects 30% YoY data growth and unpredictable per-tenant query bursts. They run on BigQuery with on-demand pricing today and want to control cost variance and isolate noisy tenants. Which design BEST balances cost predictability and isolation?
options: Switch all workloads to a single small flat-rate slot commitment
Adopt BigQuery Editions with autoscaling reservations, assign per-tenant projects to dedicated reservations, and apply baseline + max slots per tier
Move all queries to Bigtable for predictable cost
Use materialized views for every tenant query and keep on-demand pricing
explanation: BigQuery Editions with autoscaling reservations let you commit a baseline, scale up to a cap per workload, and assign tenants to isolated reservations — the canonical multi-tenant cost-and-isolation pattern. A single small flat-rate is too rigid, Bigtable is the wrong workload, and materialized views alone don't address concurrency isolation.

text: A globally distributed mobile app expects 10x growth in writes and reads over 18 months and needs strong consistency for user-profile reads after writes. The current backend is a single-region Firestore database. Which evolution path BEST preserves consistency while scaling?
options: Add cross-region read replicas to the existing Firestore database
Migrate to a Spanner multi-region instance with regional read-write replicas
Shard the Firestore database into multiple single-region databases by user-ID prefix and route in the client
Move to Bigtable with multi-cluster routing using last-write-wins
explanation: Spanner multi-region preserves strong consistency at global scale and absorbs the projected growth without client-side sharding. Firestore does not currently expose user-managed cross-region read replicas, client-side sharding pushes consistency complexity into apps, and Bigtable's multi-cluster eventually-consistent routing breaks the read-after-write requirement.

text: A team needs to give Compute Engine VMs without external IPs the ability to reach the public internet for OS package updates. Which managed service provides this?
options: Cloud VPN
Cloud NAT
Cloud Interconnect
Direct Peering
explanation: Cloud NAT provides outbound-only internet access for private VMs without exposing them with external IPs. Cloud VPN connects VPCs to other networks, Interconnect/Direct Peering provide private connectivity to on-prem or Google services, not generic internet egress.

text: Which design pattern lets multiple service projects share a single common VPC while keeping IAM and billing separate?
options: VPC Network Peering
Shared VPC with a host project and service projects
Cloud Interconnect attachments per project
Cross-project subnet exports
explanation: Shared VPC lets a host project own subnets that service projects attach to, centralizing networking while preserving per-project IAM and billing. Peering connects independent VPCs without sharing IAM, and Interconnect/'subnet exports' aren't this pattern.

text: An enterprise has a hub VPC and 12 spoke VPCs across two regions. They need transitive private routing between spokes (peering is not transitive) with minimal operational overhead. Which design fits BEST?
options: Full mesh of VPC Network Peering between every pair of spokes
Network Connectivity Center with VPC spokes attached to a hub
HA VPN tunnels between every spoke pair
A single Shared VPC and migrate all workloads into it
explanation: Network Connectivity Center (NCC) provides a hub-and-spoke topology with transitive routing across attached VPCs, replacing the N² peering mesh. A peering full mesh is operationally expensive, VPN mesh is heavier still, and consolidating into one Shared VPC may not match team or billing boundaries.

text: An application in `us-east1` makes thousands of small calls to a private Cloud SQL instance in `europe-west1`. Latency is the primary concern. What is the BEST mitigation?
options: Add Cloud CDN in front of the private Cloud SQL instance so frequently-issued queries are cached close to the application tier
Move the Cloud SQL instance (or a read replica) into us-east1 to remove transcontinental round-trips
Switch the VPC routing mode to global so cross-region traffic stays internal to Google's backbone instead of leaving the VPC
Increase the Cloud SQL machine size to a higher CPU/memory tier so each query completes faster on the database server side
explanation: Latency between continents is bounded by physics; the only durable fix is to co-locate compute and the database (move primary or add a regional read replica). Cloud CDN does not cache database queries, global VPC routing doesn't shorten geographic distance, and a bigger machine doesn't reduce round-trip time.

text: A company connects its on-prem data center to GCP via two 10 Gbps Dedicated Interconnect links in different metro locations. They need an SLA-backed topology. What is the MINIMUM supported configuration?
options: One Interconnect VLAN attachment terminated on a single Cloud Router with one BGP session in a single edge availability domain
Two attachments in two edge availability domains across two metros, two Cloud Routers, BGP on both, configured per the 99.99% SLA topology
A single VLAN attachment over a Partner Interconnect with one Cloud Router and one BGP session for the on-prem peer
One Dedicated Interconnect attachment in one metro plus a pair of HA VPN tunnels as the primary failover path for the link
explanation: The 99.99% Dedicated Interconnect topology requires attachments in two distinct edge-availability domains in two metros, each terminating on its own Cloud Router with BGP. A single attachment yields no SLA, single Partner Interconnect doesn't reach 99.99%, and adding VPN doesn't change the Interconnect SLA tier.

text: A platform team needs centralized egress filtering and inspection for all VPCs across the organization, with the ability to enforce per-FQDN allowlists for outbound traffic. Which design is MOST appropriate?
options: Cloud NAT with VPC flow logs enabled at the central transit VPC, reviewed weekly by the security team for unexpected destinations
Cloud Next Generation Firewall Enterprise with intrusion prevention and FQDN-based egress policies attached via hierarchical firewall policies
Per-VM iptables rules pushed by the configuration management agent, with FQDN allowlists rendered from a central source of truth
Organization Policy compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny so workloads cannot reach public internet destinations from VM external IPs
explanation: Cloud NGFW Enterprise adds intrusion prevention and supports FQDN/URL-based egress policy enforced through hierarchical firewall policies attached at folder/VPC scope. Cloud NAT only logs, per-VM iptables doesn't scale, and the org policy only blocks external IPs (it doesn't inspect traffic).

text: A workload needs the highest IOPS available on Google Cloud for a single VM (sub-millisecond latency). Which storage choice is BEST?
options: Standard persistent disk (pd-standard)
Local SSD attached to the VM
Filestore Basic HDD
Cloud Storage Standard
explanation: Local SSDs are physically attached to the host and offer the highest IOPS and lowest latency on GCE, at the cost of being ephemeral. Persistent disks are network-attached (lower IOPS, higher latency), Filestore Basic HDD is throughput-optimized, and GCS is object storage with much higher latency.

text: Which Cloud Storage feature automatically transitions objects from Standard to Coldline after 90 days?
options: Object Versioning
Object lifecycle management
Bucket Lock
Requester Pays
explanation: Object lifecycle management lets you define rules that change storage class or delete objects based on age and other conditions. Versioning preserves overwrites, Bucket Lock enforces retention, and Requester Pays shifts download cost to callers.

text: A PostgreSQL workload on GCE needs durable storage with up to 100,000 random read IOPS, balanced cost, and the ability to grow to 64 TB. Which disk type is BEST?
options: pd-standard
pd-balanced
Hyperdisk Extreme
Local SSD
explanation: Hyperdisk Extreme is the right tier when you need to provision very high IOPS independently of capacity for performance-critical databases. pd-balanced caps below this performance, pd-standard is HDD-class, and Local SSD is non-durable (lost on stop).

text: A team needs a network-attached file system mounted by 200 GKE pods that supports POSIX semantics and concurrent shared writes. Which option is BEST?
options: A regional Persistent Disk attached in Read-Many (multi-reader) mode to every node hosting the 200 pods needing the share
Filestore (with the GKE CSI driver) providing an NFS share
Cloud Storage FUSE mounted on each pod with write caching enabled and the same bucket prefix shared across all writers
Local SSD provisioned per node, exposed to pods via hostPath, and synchronized across nodes with a sidecar replicator
explanation: Filestore is the managed NFS service and the recommended POSIX shared-write option for GKE via its CSI driver. PD multi-writer is read-only beyond a couple of writers, GCS FUSE is not fully POSIX-compliant for concurrent writes, and Local SSD is per-node not shared.

text: An HPC team needs a shared parallel file system delivering hundreds of GB/s of aggregate throughput to thousands of GCE compute nodes for a tightly-coupled simulation. Which storage option fits BEST?
options: Filestore Enterprise tier
Parallelstore (managed parallel file system based on DAOS)
Cloud Storage with parallel composite uploads
Hyperdisk Throughput attached to each VM
explanation: Parallelstore is Google's managed high-performance parallel file system designed for HPC/AI workloads needing very high aggregate throughput across many clients. Filestore Enterprise tops out far below that, GCS is object storage (not POSIX/parallel), and Hyperdisk is per-VM attached storage.

text: A media company stores 5 PB of finished assets in GCS Standard. They access ≈ 1% of the catalog daily and the rest rarely. They want lowest cost without breaking access patterns and tolerate ~30s retrieval for cold objects. Which strategy is BEST?
options: Move the entire 5 PB catalog to the Archive storage class immediately to minimize at-rest cost regardless of access pattern
Enable Autoclass on the bucket so objects move to colder classes based on access patterns automatically
Author a single lifecycle rule that transitions every object to Coldline at age 0 and accept the early-access fees
Shard the catalog across many regional buckets organized by asset hash prefix to reduce per-bucket object count
explanation: Autoclass automatically promotes/demotes objects across Standard→Nearline→Coldline→Archive based on observed access, optimizing cost without manual lifecycle policy maintenance for mixed access patterns. Forcing all to Archive incurs early-deletion fees and slow retrieval, blanket Coldline overcharges hot objects, and bucket sharding doesn't change per-object cost.

text: Which Compute Engine machine family is recommended as the default general-purpose choice for cost-balanced web/app workloads with the latest Intel processors?
options: E2
C3
N2D
M3
explanation: C3 is Google's latest Intel-based general-purpose family with strong price-performance for web/app workloads. E2 is cost-optimized but older and shared-core; N2D is AMD; M3 is memory-optimized.

text: Which feature lets a Compute Engine VM continue running through host maintenance events without restart?
