options: Live migration (default for general-purpose VMs)
Preemptible VMs
Sole-tenant nodes
Confidential VMs always require restart
explanation: Live migration moves running VMs to another host during maintenance with no reboot — the default for general-purpose families. Preemptible/Spot VMs are explicitly stoppable, sole-tenant is for hardware isolation, and Confidential VMs do support live migration on supported families.

text: A team's stateless workload runs fine on N2 VMs but they want a 20–40% price/performance improvement and are willing to switch CPU architecture. Which choice is MOST appropriate?
options: Switch the MIG to E2 shared-core machine types and rely on the cost-optimized scheduling to absorb peak load
Switch to T2A or C4A (Google Axion / Arm) and rebuild the container for arm64
Move the workload to M3 memory-optimized VMs to take advantage of the higher per-core memory ratio
Place the existing N2 VMs on sole-tenant nodes so the workload gets dedicated hardware with no noisy neighbors
explanation: Arm-based families (T2A and the newer Google Axion C4A) typically offer 20–40% price/performance improvements for stateless cloud-native workloads after a multi-arch container rebuild. E2 trades performance for cost, M3 is memory-optimized (different goal), and sole-tenant is about isolation, not performance.

text: A team running a Cloud Run service finds CPU is being throttled outside of request handling, breaking background callbacks. Which configuration change is the simplest fix?
options: Switch the workload to GKE Autopilot so background goroutines keep running on always-allocated pod CPU outside request handling
Enable CPU always-allocated (instance-based billing) on the Cloud Run service so background work runs between requests
Increase request concurrency to 1000 so more requests overlap and the instance stays warm enough for background callbacks
Switch to Cloud Run jobs and trigger the background callbacks on a Cloud Scheduler cron at the desired callback frequency
explanation: Cloud Run's default 'CPU only during requests' throttles work between requests; enabling instance-based CPU allocation lets background tasks keep running. More concurrency does not help, switching to jobs changes the execution model (one-shot, no requests), and moving to GKE is far heavier.

text: An ML team needs a Compute Engine GPU instance for training that uses NVIDIA H100 with NVLink and high-speed GPU-to-GPU bandwidth. Which choice is BEST?
options: G2 instances (NVIDIA L4)
A3 instances (NVIDIA H100 with NVLink/NVSwitch)
N1 with attached T4 GPUs
C3 with TPU v5e attached
explanation: A3 VMs are Google's H100-based platform with NVLink/NVSwitch GPU interconnect, designed for large-model training. G2 (L4) and N1+T4 are inference-focused and lack NVLink, and TPUs are attached to TPU VMs, not C3.

text: A regulated workload requires that no other tenant runs on the same physical host AND that the OS license is brought from on-prem. Which configuration BEST satisfies this?
options: Confidential VM with Shielded VM enabled
Sole-tenant nodes with bring-your-own-license
GKE Autopilot with `securityContext.runAsNonRoot`
Spot VMs with custom images
explanation: Sole-tenant nodes give you a dedicated physical host (no neighbors) and explicitly support BYOL for OS licenses. Confidential VMs encrypt memory but share hosts, Autopilot enforces pod-level security but not host isolation, and Spot VMs are about cost/preemption.

text: Which BigQuery feature lets you keep tables of the same schema partitioned by date so that queries scan only the relevant partitions?
options: Clustering by hash
Date/timestamp partitioning
Materialized views
Authorized views
explanation: Partitioning by ingestion time, date, or integer range prunes scans to the relevant partitions, reducing cost and latency. Clustering further optimizes within partitions, materialized views precompute aggregates, and authorized views are an access-control pattern.

text: Where does GCS data physically reside if a bucket is created with the location `US` (multi-region)?
options: In a single specific zone within the us-central1 region, replicated synchronously to two other zones inside that region
Across multiple regions within the United States, with redundancy across them
In every Google Cloud region globally, with the closest region serving each request and lazy replication elsewhere
Only in customer-selected on-prem locations connected via Google Distributed Cloud Edge appliances managed in the project
explanation: A `US` multi-region bucket stores data redundantly across multiple US regions, providing higher availability than a single region. It is not single-zone, not all-global, and certainly not on-prem.

text: A regulated workload requires that BigQuery query results, intermediate files, and exports stay within the EU. Which configuration is REQUIRED?
options: Create the dataset in the `EU` multi-region (or a specific EU region) and only run queries from clients that submit jobs in that region
Use any region for the dataset but enable CMEK with EU-located keys so encryption operations happen inside EU key rings
Create the dataset in `US` and apply IAM Conditions limiting query access to principals whose Workspace account is in an EU country
Export the result of every BigQuery job to a Cloud Storage bucket in the EU and have applications read only from that bucket
explanation: BigQuery query jobs and storage are bound to the dataset's location; the only way to keep all processing inside the EU is to locate the dataset there and submit jobs to that location. CMEK key location and IAM restrictions don't change data residency, and exports happen after processing has already occurred.

text: A team needs to share a curated subset of a BigQuery dataset with an external partner across organizations, without copying data and with separate billing for the consumer. Which feature fits BEST?
options: Authorized views in the producer's project
Analytics Hub (BigQuery sharing) with a published listing
Scheduled queries that export CSVs to a shared bucket
Granting the partner the BigQuery Data Owner role on the dataset
explanation: Analytics Hub publishes a curated dataset listing that consumers subscribe to in their own projects — no copy, with consumer-side query billing. Authorized views work within a single org/project boundary, scheduled exports duplicate data, and granting Data Owner is overpermissive and bills the producer.

text: An organization needs a unified governance layer over data in BigQuery and GCS — lineage, classification, fine-grained access via attribute-based tags, and a central data catalog — across many projects. Which service should be adopted?
options: The legacy standalone Data Catalog service operated independently of Dataplex, with manual tag templates per project
Dataplex Universal Catalog (with policy tags and lineage)
Cloud DLP (Sensitive Data Protection) inspection jobs scheduled per bucket to classify objects by sensitive-data type
BigQuery row-level security policies applied per dataset to restrict which rows individual principals can read in a query
explanation: Dataplex Universal Catalog unifies cataloging, lineage, classification, and policy-tag-based access control across GCS and BigQuery. Legacy Data Catalog is being subsumed by Dataplex, DLP is content inspection (not governance), and BigQuery row-level security is a single feature within one product.

text: A BigQuery dataset has tables that are append-only with a typical query pattern of `WHERE event_date BETWEEN ... AND user_id = ...`. Tables are 10 TB. Which physical design BEST reduces scan cost?
options: Partition by `event_date` and cluster by `user_id`
Partition by `user_id` modulo 100
Materialize one table per user
No optimization needed; on-demand billing handles it
explanation: Date partitioning prunes by `event_date`, and clustering by `user_id` colocates rows so the per-partition scan reads fewer blocks — the canonical pattern for time-range + filtered-id queries. Per-user partitioning explodes partition count, table-per-user is unmanageable, and ignoring physical design defeats the cost goal.

text: Which IAM principle states that users and services should be granted only the permissions they actually need?
options: Defense in depth
Least privilege
Separation of duties
Zero trust
explanation: Least privilege grants only the permissions required for a task, minimizing blast radius. Defense in depth, separation of duties, and zero trust are related security principles but describe different concepts.

text: Which feature lets a service running on GCE call other Google APIs without managing static keys?
options: Generate per-VM JSON service-account key files at provisioning time and ship them to each instance via Secret Manager
An attached service account on the VM, used via the metadata server (default credentials)
Embed an OAuth client ID and client secret in the instance startup script and exchange them for tokens on each boot
Bake an end-user's gcloud refresh token into the custom image so every VM authenticates as that human identity
explanation: Attaching a service account to a VM lets workloads obtain short-lived tokens from the metadata server — no static keys to manage or rotate. Static JSON keys, OAuth client secrets, and embedded user credentials are all anti-patterns.

text: A GitHub Actions pipeline needs to deploy to Google Cloud without storing a long-lived service-account key as a GitHub secret. What is the recommended pattern?
options: Generate a service-account key, store it as a GitHub secret, and rotate it monthly
Configure Workload Identity Federation with GitHub OIDC and impersonate a service account from the workflow
Use a personal access token of an organization owner
Embed the key in the GitHub repository as an encrypted file
explanation: Workload Identity Federation lets external OIDC identities (like GitHub Actions) directly impersonate a service account, eliminating long-lived keys. Storing or embedding keys, or relying on user PATs, are exactly the patterns WIF replaces.

text: A platform team needs to grant a contractor write access to a single GCS bucket, but ONLY when accessed from corporate IPs and only for the next 30 days. Which IAM construct fits BEST?
options: Add the contractor as Storage Object Admin at the project level so they can write to the bucket along with their other working buckets
Grant Storage Object Admin on the bucket with an IAM Condition restricting request.time and request.ip via Access Context Manager
Create a service account, generate and email a long-lived JSON key to the contractor, and rotate it manually after the engagement ends
Add the contractor to a custom IAM role at the organization level so the same identity can be reused for any future engagements
explanation: IAM Conditions support time-bound and (via Access Context Manager) network-restricted bindings on a single resource — exactly the requested constraints. Project-level admin is too broad, sharing keys is an anti-pattern, and an org-level custom role is way over-scoped.

text: By default, what encryption is applied to data stored in Google Cloud?
options: Customer must provide their own keys before any data can be written
Google-managed encryption at rest with AES-256, applied by default
Encryption is opt-in via Cloud KMS only
Data is encrypted only in transit, not at rest
explanation: Google encrypts customer data at rest by default with AES-256, no configuration required. CMEK and CSEK are opt-in upgrades for additional control; data is also encrypted in transit by default.

text: Which Google Cloud service is used to manage symmetric and asymmetric cryptographic keys with rotation, IAM, and integration into other services?
options: Secret Manager
Cloud KMS
Cloud HSM (only)
Certificate Authority Service
explanation: Cloud KMS provides managed cryptographic keys with rotation, IAM, and integration via CMEK across many services. Secret Manager stores secrets (not keys), Cloud HSM is a key-protection backend within KMS, and CA Service issues certificates.

text: A regulated workload requires that the customer holds and rotates the encryption keys used to encrypt GCS objects, with an auditable trail when Google services use those keys. Which option fits BEST?
options: Default Google-managed keys
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) in Cloud KMS, with key access logged via Cloud Audit Logs
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) provided on every request only
Application-layer encryption with keys in environment variables
explanation: CMEK gives customers ownership and rotation of the keys while letting GCS use them transparently; KMS access is recorded in Cloud Audit Logs. Default keys offer no customer control, CSEK requires per-request key supply (operationally fragile), and env-var keys defeat KMS protections.

text: A team must store API tokens and database passwords for use by Cloud Run services with versioning, audit, and IAM-controlled access. Which service is the canonical fit?
options: Cloud KMS
Secret Manager
Firestore document with field-level encryption
Cloud Storage object with `private` ACL
explanation: Secret Manager is purpose-built for storing application secrets with versioning, IAM, audit logs, and Cloud Run integration. KMS manages keys (not secrets directly), Firestore is a database not a secret store, and ACL'd GCS objects lack versioned secret semantics and rotation tooling.

text: A bank requires that the encryption keys used for sensitive data NEVER leave a FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware boundary controlled by Google. Which option satisfies this?
options: Software-protected CMEK in Cloud KMS
Cloud HSM keys (FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware-backed) used as CMEK
External Key Manager (EKM) without hardware backing
Default Google encryption only
explanation: Cloud HSM provides hardware-backed keys validated to FIPS 140-2 Level 3, used transparently as CMEK across supported services. Software-protected KMS is FIPS 140-2 Level 1, EKM hosts keys outside Google entirely (different requirement), and default encryption gives no customer key control.

text: An architect needs to ensure that even if Google were compelled to disclose data, no plaintext could be recovered without a key held outside Google's control. Which design fits BEST?
options: CMEK in Cloud KMS with software protection so the wrapping key never leaves the Google-operated KMS service
Cloud External Key Manager (EKM) with keys held by a third-party key partner outside Google
Confidential VMs with default Google-managed encryption so workload memory is encrypted with hardware-isolated keys
Secret Manager with replication policy automatic and CMEK so the application secrets stay protected at rest in every region
explanation: Cloud EKM stores keys in an external third-party key manager so Google cannot decrypt without that external authorization. CMEK keys still live in Google KMS, Confidential VMs encrypt RAM but use Google-controlled keys, and Secret Manager doesn't change the encryption boundary.

text: Which Google Cloud feature lets an organization enforce a centrally-defined rule across all projects, such as 'disallow VMs with external IPs'?
options: IAM custom roles
Organization Policy Service constraints
Cloud Audit Logs
Project labels
explanation: Organization Policy Service applies constraints (e.g., `compute.vmExternalIpAccess`) inheritably down the resource hierarchy. IAM grants permissions but doesn't enforce structural constraints, audit logs only record events, and labels are metadata.

text: Which Google Cloud service surfaces actionable security findings (misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, threats) across an organization in one place?
options: Cloud Logging
Security Command Center
Cloud Asset Inventory
Recommender
explanation: Security Command Center (SCC) aggregates security findings from built-in and integrated services across the org. Cloud Logging stores logs, Asset Inventory lists resources/IAM, and Recommender suggests cost/perf optimizations.

text: A regulated workload must be limited to specific GCP services AND prevented from exfiltrating data into other projects via service APIs. Which control is the canonical fit?
options: VPC firewall egress rules denying outbound traffic to all IP ranges except an allowlist maintained by the network security team
VPC Service Controls perimeter around the projects, restricting service access and cross-perimeter data movement
An Organization Policy disallowing external IP addresses on Compute Engine instances and Cloud Run services across the org
IAM Conditions on each service-enabling binding using `request.host` to gate access by the requesting hostname per call
explanation: VPC Service Controls create a service perimeter that prevents API-level data movement out of the bounded projects — the standard control for API-based exfiltration. Network firewall rules don't see service-API context, org policy on external IPs only blocks public IPs, and IAM Conditions can't enforce cross-project data movement at the service layer.

text: Which combination provides PCI DSS-aligned scope reduction for a payments microservice handling cardholder data on GCP?
options: Place the service in a dedicated project + folder, apply VPC Service Controls, restrict IAM to a small set of principals, and isolate data in a separate KMS keyring
Use a single shared project with all workloads and rely on labels to mark PCI workloads
Disable Cloud Logging to reduce auditor scope
Use sole-tenant nodes only
explanation: Project/folder isolation, perimeter controls, restricted IAM, and dedicated key material together reduce PCI scope by clearly bounding cardholder-data systems. Sharing a project, disabling logging, and sole-tenant alone do not establish the scope boundary auditors require.

text: A FedRAMP High customer requires US-only personnel and US-only data handling for a workload, with cryptographic and operational guardrails enforced by Google. Which offering is designed for this?
options: Standard Google Cloud regions in the US with CMEK
Assured Workloads for US Regions / FedRAMP High control package
Sovereign cloud partner only
Anthos on bare metal
explanation: Assured Workloads enforces FedRAMP High data residency and personnel constraints with built-in policy guardrails. Vanilla regions plus CMEK do not enforce personnel or operational scope; sovereign partners and Anthos solve other problems.

text: A team must continuously detect IAM drift (new owner grants, deleted policies) across a 200-project org and trigger remediation workflows. Which architecture fits BEST?
options: Have the security team perform a quarterly manual review of IAM policies in the console for each of the 200 projects
Use Cloud Asset Inventory feeds for IAM-policy changes to Pub/Sub, processed by Cloud Functions / Workflows, with Security Command Center for centralized findings
Configure per-project log-based metrics on SetIamPolicy events and rely on per-project alerting for remediation
Disable IAM policy changes for everyone except the security team and require a ticket for every grant or revocation request
explanation: Cloud Asset Inventory feeds emit IAM-policy changes in near-real-time to Pub/Sub; downstream automation evaluates and remediates while SCC centralizes findings — the canonical org-wide drift-detection pattern. Manual review doesn't scale, log-based metrics alone don't carry policy structure, and blanket-blocking IAM changes prevents legitimate work.

text: Which Google Cloud service is the managed CI/CD platform for building and deploying to GKE, Cloud Run, and GCE?
options: Cloud Deploy
Cloud Build
Cloud Source Repositories
Artifact Registry
explanation: Cloud Build executes build, test, and deploy pipelines on GCP. Cloud Deploy is the managed continuous-delivery (release-promotion) layer that often invokes Cloud Build artifacts; Source Repos hosts code; Artifact Registry stores artifacts.

text: Which Google Cloud service stores container images, Maven, npm, Python, and other artifacts in regional repositories?
options: Cloud Storage buckets only
Artifact Registry
Container Registry (recommended for new projects)
Cloud Source Repositories
explanation: Artifact Registry is Google's recommended artifact store for containers and language packages. Container Registry is a deprecated predecessor, GCS is generic storage, and Source Repos hosts code.

text: A team wants standardized release promotion across dev→staging→prod for a Cloud Run service, with manual approval at the prod gate and automatic rollback on metric breach. Which service is purpose-built for this?
options: Cloud Build only, with branch triggers
Cloud Deploy with delivery pipelines, targets, and approvals
Cloud Tasks queues
Workflows
explanation: Cloud Deploy provides first-class delivery pipelines with promotion stages, approvals, and rollback for GKE, Cloud Run, and GCE. Cloud Build can stitch this together but lacks the release-management primitives; Tasks/Workflows are general orchestration tools.

text: An organization wants their CI to enforce that only images built by a trusted build system can be deployed to production GKE clusters. Which combination provides this?
options: Cloud Build producing signed attestations + Binary Authorization policy on GKE requiring those attestations
Cloud Build with workload identity only
Manual code review and a merge-only branch
Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning enabled
explanation: Binary Authorization enforces deploy-time policies that admit only images carrying valid attestations — typically produced by trusted CI like Cloud Build. WIF, code review, and vulnerability scanning improve security but don't enforce the deploy-time admission rule.

text: An enterprise wants SLSA Level 3 build provenance for container images deployed to production. Which Google Cloud capability is the canonical fit?
options: Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning
Cloud Build with built-in SLSA Level 3 build provenance and Software Delivery Shield (Binary Authorization, Assured OSS)
Cloud Deploy approvals only
GCS object signing
explanation: Cloud Build emits SLSA Level 3 provenance for container builds, and Software Delivery Shield ties together provenance, Binary Authorization, and Assured OSS for end-to-end supply chain integrity. Vulnerability scanning, deploy approvals, and GCS signing each address narrower problems.

text: A platform team must enforce that infrastructure changes flow through code review, are previewed before apply, and that drift from declared state is detected. Which design fits BEST on GCP?
options: Have privileged engineers run gcloud commands manually from their workstations and capture the output in a shared change log
PR-driven Terraform plans run via Cloud Build / Infrastructure Manager, applied with an attached service account, with periodic drift checks
Allow direct console edits in production projects and audit them after the fact via Cloud Audit Logs and weekly review meetings
Maintain a spreadsheet of approved configurations and require platform engineers to reconcile each project against it each quarter
explanation: PR-driven Terraform with Cloud Build (or Infrastructure Manager) gives reviewable plans, controlled apply, and supports periodic drift detection via re-plans. Manual CLI, console edits, and spreadsheets are the anti-patterns that this control model exists to replace.

text: Which Google Cloud feature lets a finance team see daily project costs broken out by service, label, and SKU?
options: Cloud Monitoring dashboards
Cloud Billing reports and BigQuery billing export
Cloud Logging metrics
Recommender
explanation: Cloud Billing provides reports in the console and a BigQuery billing export for ad-hoc analysis by service/label/SKU. Monitoring, Logging, and Recommender don't carry billing data at this granularity.

text: Which feature notifies an owner when a project's spend approaches a defined threshold for the month?
options: Cloud Billing budgets and alerts
Cloud Monitoring uptime checks
Recommender
Cloud Logging sinks
explanation: Budgets and alerts in Cloud Billing send Pub/Sub or email notifications at configurable percentage thresholds. Uptime checks, Recommender, and log sinks address different concerns.

text: A team running a steady 24/7 GCE workload wants to reduce its compute bill by ~30–55% with a multi-year commitment but retain the ability to change machine families within the family group. Which option is BEST?
options: Spot VMs sized to the steady-state load, accepting the ~30s preemption notice and rebuilding state from durable storage
Resource-based committed use discounts (1- or 3-year)
Sustained use discounts only, applied automatically to whichever instances happen to run for most of each billing month
Sole-tenant nodes provisioned to match the workload's vCPU and memory footprint, with no committed-use commitment attached
explanation: Resource-based CUDs deliver 30–55%+ savings for steady-state usage with 1- or 3-year terms, and flex CUDs allow changes within a family group. Spot is for interruptible workloads, SUDs apply automatically and yield smaller savings, and sole-tenant is about isolation, not savings.

text: Which approach BEST attributes shared platform costs (logging, monitoring, networking) to consuming product teams?
options: Run every project from a single shared Google Cloud project with no labels and reconcile costs in a quarterly spreadsheet
Per-team folders/projects with mandatory team and cost-center labels enforced by Org Policy, queried from BigQuery billing-export by label
Have the FinOps team produce monthly cost-attribution spreadsheets by hand from the console billing reports and circulate them
Use a single Cloud Billing account split arbitrarily across teams by manual percentage at the end of each billing month
explanation: Folder/project structure plus enforced labels feeding BigQuery billing-export queries gives granular, auditable showback/chargeback. Single projects, manual spreadsheets, and arbitrary splits all break attribution.

text: A platform owner wants to safely apply Recommender suggestions (idle VMs, oversized machines) at scale without manual approvals for low-risk recommendations. Which design fits BEST?
options: Have the on-call FinOps engineer manually click each Recommender suggestion in the console after a daily review session
Read Recommender programmatically via API/Asset Inventory, classify by risk, auto-apply low-risk via a workflow with audit logs, queue higher-risk for review
Disable Recommender at the organization level so the team is not distracted by its noisy suggestions during normal operations
Apply every Recommender suggestion immediately at scale, including production downsizing, to maximize realized cost savings
explanation: Reading Recommender programmatically and gating by risk class is the canonical FinOps automation pattern, with auditability via Cloud Audit Logs. Manual-only doesn't scale, disabling forfeits savings, and blanket auto-apply is dangerous in production.

text: An organization signs a Google Cloud commitment with a baseline spend across BigQuery slots and Compute Engine. They want to optimize portfolio-wide commitment usage and avoid stranded capacity. Which approach is BEST?
options: Dedicate fixed CUD blocks per team and prohibit reallocation
Use Billing accounts with consolidated reporting + flex CUDs/Editions autoscaling reservations + a periodic FinOps review using Recommender and Billing exports
Buy 3-year resource-based CUDs for every workload regardless of stability
Use only Spot VMs to avoid commitment risk
explanation: Consolidated billing + flexible commitments (flex CUDs and BigQuery autoscaling reservations) + recurring FinOps reviews lets the org rebalance commitments to actual usage. Locking commitments per team strands capacity, blanket 3-year CUDs is risky for variable workloads, and Spot-only forfeits steady-state savings.

text: Which Google framework provides best-practice guidance organized by reliability, security, cost, and performance pillars for cloud workloads?
options: AWS Well-Architected Framework
Google Cloud Architecture Framework
ITIL v4
TOGAF
explanation: The Google Cloud Architecture Framework is Google's pillar-based best-practice guidance. AWS has its own framework, and ITIL/TOGAF are industry frameworks not specific to GCP.

text: Which role typically owns long-term cloud cost optimization, governance, and engineering enablement across an enterprise?
options: The IT help desk team that handles end-user account password resets and laptop hardware tickets across the organization
Cloud Center of Excellence (CCoE) / Platform team
A single database administrator group focused on schema design and performance tuning for the production database fleet
The owner of one Google Cloud project, responsible only for the resources and IAM bindings inside that single project
explanation: A CCoE/Platform team is the standard organizational pattern that owns multi-team cloud governance, FinOps, paved paths, and enablement. Help desk, DBA, and project owner roles have narrower scope.

text: A startup wants to enable many small teams to ship to GCP independently while keeping a central security baseline. Which pattern is MOST appropriate?
options: One enormous shared project for all teams
Folders per business unit, projects per workload, organization policies for the security baseline, and a shared 'platform' project for common infrastructure
Each team uses its own personal Google account and pays with a credit card
Single-region single-project deployment with IAM-only separation
explanation: The recommended landing-zone pattern uses folders for org structure, per-workload projects for blast-radius isolation, org policies for the security baseline, and a shared platform project for common infrastructure. The other options either over-share or fragment governance.

text: Which practice helps an organization moving to GCP avoid 'snowflake' projects with one-off configurations?
options: Free-form clicks in the console
Reusable infrastructure-as-code modules + a project-vending machine that provisions standard projects on request
Per-team Terraform modules with no central standards
Disabling Organization Policy
explanation: Project-factory / vending-machine patterns built on IaC modules ensure every new project starts from the same baseline. Console clicks, fragmented modules, and disabling org policy all create the snowflakes the question asks how to avoid.

text: A leadership team is unsure whether to refactor a legacy on-prem app or lift-and-shift it to GCE first. Which advice is consistent with Google's migration guidance?
options: Always refactor every workload first; lift-and-shift to GCE is never appropriate for a serious enterprise migration program
Use the 7Rs to classify each workload by business priority and technical fit, often starting with rehost for speed and refactoring in later waves
Always lift-and-shift every workload to GCE first; refactoring is not necessary once the workload is running on Google Cloud
Skip the migration planning phase and move workloads to Google Cloud in alphabetical order by application name
explanation: Google's migration guidance recommends classifying workloads by the 7Rs and choosing the right strategy per workload, often starting with rehost to gain speed and modernizing in waves. Both 'always refactor' and 'always rehost' ignore workload context, and unplanned migration is a recipe for failure.

text: An enterprise wants to mature its cloud adoption from 'tactical' (per-team experiments) to 'transformational' (org-wide capability). Per Google's Cloud Adoption Framework, which combination of investments BEST drives that maturity shift?
options: Buy more 3-year committed-use discounts to lock in pricing and let teams self-organize around the resulting capacity allocations
Establish a CCoE; standardize landing zones and golden paths; adopt FinOps; build SRE practices; sponsor org-wide training and change management
Switch the org to a multi-cloud strategy without a workload-fit assessment to maximize negotiating leverage with each provider
Move all newly-cloud workloads back on-prem so the org can re-run the adoption process with the lessons learned from the tactical phase
explanation: The Cloud Adoption Framework calls out people (CCoE, training), process (FinOps, SRE), and technology (landing zones, golden paths) as the levers that move organizations from tactical to transformational. The other options are not maturity-shifting investments.

text: A risk-averse customer is debating multi-cloud vs single-cloud for a new platform. Which advice aligns with Google's published guidance on portability and resilience?
options: Always go multi-cloud from day one to avoid vendor lock-in, even when no business driver requires it
Prefer single-cloud with portable abstractions (containers, open standards); use multi-cloud (Anthos / GDC) only for clear drivers like data sovereignty, M&A, or best-of-breed services
Standardize on a single cloud forever and forbid any workload from running in another provider under any circumstance
Run identical active-active copies of every workload across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud to maximize resilience
explanation: Google's guidance is to avoid multi-cloud complexity unless concrete drivers justify it, while keeping workloads portable via containers/open APIs and using Anthos/GDC for the cases where multi-environment is genuinely required. The 'always' and 'never' positions, and triple-running everywhere, are not aligned with that guidance.

text: Which CLI is the primary interface for managing most Google Cloud resources from a terminal or script?
options: `aws`
`gcloud` (Google Cloud SDK)
`kubectl` only
`terraform` only
explanation: `gcloud` is the official Google Cloud CLI for resource management and scripting. `aws` is for AWS, `kubectl` is for Kubernetes specifically, and Terraform is an IaC tool that calls underlying APIs.

text: Which interactive shell environment lets you run `gcloud` and `kubectl` from a browser with no local install?
options: Cloud Shell
Local Bash on the user's laptop
BigQuery Studio
Apigee proxy
explanation: Cloud Shell provides a browser-based Linux shell with the SDK pre-installed and a small persistent home directory. The other options are not browser-based GCP shells.

text: A team needs to call Google Cloud APIs from many environments (CI, on-prem, partner clouds) without long-lived service-account keys. Which authentication mechanism is recommended?
options: API keys hard-coded into the application source repository and rotated quarterly via a manual pull request
Application Default Credentials backed by Workload Identity Federation
User OAuth refresh tokens generated by an engineer locally and shared with each environment via email and chat
Per-environment HTTP Basic auth credentials stored in plaintext config files and read by the application at startup
explanation: ADC + WIF is the recommended pattern for keyless authentication from external workloads, supported across many providers. The other options are exactly the long-lived-credential anti-patterns WIF replaces.

text: Which approach BEST organizes large-scale `gcloud` automation for repeatable, reviewable infrastructure changes across multiple projects?
options: Bash scripts copied between machines
Terraform (or Infrastructure Manager) source code in version control with PR review and CI/CD execution
Manual console clicks recorded in Word documents
Per-engineer ad-hoc gcloud commands
explanation: IaC under version control with PR review and CI/CD execution is Google's recommended way to scale repeatable, reviewable infra changes. Bash scripts, console clicks, and ad-hoc commands all break repeatability and audit.

text: A regulated team needs API access from on-prem to Google APIs that does NOT traverse the public internet. Which solution fits BEST?
options: Public APIs over Cloud NAT
Private Google Access for on-premises hosts via Interconnect/VPN, hitting `private.googleapis.com` or `restricted.googleapis.com`
Whitelist source IPs in IAM
Use a personal proxy on the engineer's laptop
explanation: Private Google Access for on-premises hosts (with the `private`/`restricted` API endpoints) routes API calls over Interconnect/VPN to Google's private network, never the public internet. NAT and IAM IP allowlists don't change the network path; a personal proxy is not a sanctioned solution.

text: A central security team wants every project's API calls auditable, with admin activity logs immutable and accessible for at least 7 years across the organization. Which design fits BEST?
options: Rely on the default per-project Cloud Logging retention with no aggregation and no immutability controls applied at the org level
Aggregated org-level log sink routing Admin Activity logs to a Cloud Storage bucket with Bucket Lock 7-year retention (or BigQuery with retention)
Have an admin manually screenshot the console activity pages periodically and store the screenshots in a shared drive folder
Disable Cloud Audit Logs across the organization to reduce log-storage cost and the resulting BigQuery query expense
explanation: Org-level aggregated sinks centralize logs across all projects, and Bucket Lock makes retention immutable for the required period. Default per-project retention is too short and not centralized; screenshots aren't durable; disabling audit logs is non-compliant.

text: Which Google Cloud service collects logs from GCP services and user applications and provides a unified query interface?
options: Cloud Logging
Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Run
explanation: Cloud Logging ingests platform and application logs and provides a unified Logs Explorer. Trace is for latency spans, Profiler is for CPU/heap profiles, and Cloud Run is a compute platform.

text: Which service stores time-series metrics from GCP services and lets you build dashboards and alerts?
options: Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Logging
Cloud Source Repositories
Pub/Sub
explanation: Cloud Monitoring is Google's managed metrics, dashboards, and alerting service. Logging is for log data, Source Repos hosts code, and Pub/Sub is messaging.

text: An SRE team wants to alert when an HTTP service's 5xx error rate exceeds 1% over a 5-minute window. Which Cloud Monitoring construct is the right fit?
options: Configure a Cloud Monitoring uptime check that hits the service homepage every minute and alert on consecutive failures
Alerting policy on a metric-ratio (5xx requests / total requests) with a 1% threshold over 5 minutes, sent to a notification channel
Use a Cloud Logging filter that captures 5xx log entries and have the on-call review the saved query each morning
Configure a Cloud Billing budget alert at the project level so spend spikes from retried error responses surface to the team
explanation: Alerting policies on metric ratios let you express SLO-style error-rate alerts directly. Uptime checks measure reachability not error rate, log entries are not alerts, and budgets are billing alerts.

text: A team needs to measure and report on a service's availability and latency SLOs in Cloud Monitoring with a burn-rate alert. Which feature should they use?
options: Custom dashboard widgets only
Service monitoring with SLO definitions and burn-rate alerting
Cloud Trace alone
Cloud Logging metrics with manual percentiles
explanation: Cloud Monitoring's service monitoring (formerly known as service-level monitoring) lets you define SLIs and SLOs with built-in burn-rate alerts. Dashboards visualize but don't compute SLO budgets, Trace measures spans, and log-based metrics are a manual workaround.

text: An organization needs centralized log aggregation across hundreds of projects with long-term storage and SQL-style ad-hoc analysis. Which Cloud Logging design fits BEST?
options: Default _Default sinks per project, queried separately
Aggregated organization-level log sink to a BigQuery dataset (or Log Analytics-enabled bucket) with partitioned tables and IAM controls for analysts
Manual export to GCS as JSON, queried by `grep`
Stream logs to a single project's Cloud Logging without sinks
explanation: Aggregated org-level sinks plus BigQuery (or a Log Analytics-enabled bucket) provide SQL-style multi-project log analysis with retention. Per-project defaults don't aggregate, GCS+grep is unscalable, and a single project ingest doesn't centralize across the org.

text: An online retailer wants to detect anomalous spikes in order-failure rate that don't follow a static threshold. Which approach is BEST?
options: Configure a static threshold of 5% order-failure rate that pages the on-call rotation as soon as it is breached for any 5-minute window
Cloud Monitoring with metric-absence and forecast-based / dynamic-threshold alerting (MQL or PromQL) tied to SLO burn rate
Have the SRE team manually review the order-failure dashboards once a day and open an incident if the trend looks anomalous
Disable alerting overnight to reduce paging volume and rely on the next morning's review to catch any anomalous failure spikes
explanation: SLO burn-rate plus forecast/MQL-based alerting catches anomalies that don't follow static thresholds and respects business risk. Static thresholds miss seasonality, daily review is too slow for spikes, and disabling alerts at night defeats the purpose.

text: Which deployment strategy gradually shifts a small percentage of traffic to a new version, monitors metrics, and then proceeds or rolls back?
options: Big-bang
Canary
Recreate
Direct replace
explanation: Canary deployments route a fraction of traffic to a new version and proceed only if metrics hold. Big-bang/recreate/replace deploy everything at once.

text: Which Cloud Run feature lets you route only a percentage of incoming traffic to a newly deployed revision?
options: Service accounts
Traffic splitting between revisions
VPC connector
Concurrency setting
explanation: Cloud Run's traffic splitting assigns weighted traffic to specific revisions — the canonical canary mechanism on Cloud Run. The other settings address identity, networking, and per-instance concurrency.

text: A GKE team wants progressive delivery (canary, blue/green) for many microservices with a unified rollout policy. Which managed service fits BEST?
options: Cloud Build only
Cloud Deploy with canary strategies and approvals
Cloud Source Repositories
Cloud Tasks
explanation: Cloud Deploy supports canary and other progressive strategies with managed promotion and approvals across GKE/Cloud Run/GCE targets. Cloud Build builds artifacts, Source Repos hosts code, and Tasks is general queueing.

text: Which deployment pattern keeps two complete environments (old and new) live, switches traffic atomically at the load balancer, and offers fast rollback by switching back?
options: Rolling update
Blue/Green deployment
Canary deployment
Recreate
explanation: Blue/green keeps both environments fully running and shifts traffic atomically, with rollback being a switch back. Rolling update overwrites in place; canary shifts gradually; recreate causes downtime.

text: A team needs to safely test a new ML inference revision on real production traffic, comparing its outputs to the current revision without affecting users. Which technique fits BEST?
options: Big-bang cutover at midnight from the current revision to the new ML inference revision and roll back on the first user-visible error
Shadow (mirror) traffic to the new revision and compare outputs offline; do not return its responses to users until parity is validated
Canary 50/50 between the two revisions immediately and compare aggregated quality metrics during the first hour of production use
Disable the new revision in production and rely on staging dashboards to determine whether it would have produced parity outputs
explanation: Shadow/mirror traffic replays real requests to the new version while users still receive the old version's responses, perfect for validating ML inference parity safely. Big-bang and aggressive canary expose users to the unvalidated revision; relying on dashboards alone doesn't compare outputs.

text: A platform team operating dozens of GKE clusters wants GitOps-style declarative deployments where the cluster state is reconciled to a Git repo, with policy enforcement and drift detection across fleets. Which Google offering is the canonical fit?
options: Each developer running `kubectl apply` from their laptop against the active cluster context whenever a manifest changes
Config Sync + Policy Controller (part of GKE Enterprise / Anthos Config Management)
A Cloud Build trigger per repository that applies manifests on push, with no in-cluster controller reconciling drift afterward
A jumphost VM running a scripted `helm upgrade` loop that re-installs each release on a fixed cron schedule for every cluster
explanation: Config Sync continuously reconciles cluster state from Git, and Policy Controller enforces guardrails across fleets — Google's GitOps story. Manual kubectl, ad-hoc Cloud Build, and a scripted Helm VM all lack continuous reconciliation and fleet-wide policy.
